# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Weighted Residual Method (WRM) — Full Derivation

**Given ODE:**
$$\frac{d^2 u}{dx^2} + u + x = 0, \quad 0 \le x \le 1, \quad u(0) = 0, \; u(1) = 0$$

**Step 1 — Exact Solution Check:**
The general solution of $u'' + u = -x$ is $u(x) = A\cos x + B\sin x - x$.
Applying BCs: $u(0) = 0 \Rightarrow A = 0$; $u(1) = 0 \Rightarrow B\sin 1 - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow B = \csc 1$.
Exact: $u(x) = \frac{\sin x}{\sin 1} - x$.

**Step 2 — Assume Approximate Solution Satisfying BCs (Galerkin-compatible):**
Since $u(0) = u(1) = 0$, a natural choice is $\tilde{u}(x) = a_1\,x(1-x)$. This already satisfies both BCs.

A higher-order two-term choice:
$$\tilde{u}(x) = a_1\,x(1-x) + a_2\,x^2(1-x)$$

Let us use the **two-term** approximation.

**Step 3 — Compute Residual:**
$$\frac{d\tilde{u}}{dx} = a_1(1-2x) + a_2(2x - 3x^2)$$
$$\frac{d^2\tilde{u}}{dx^2} = -2a_1 + a_2(2 - 6x)$$

Therefore:
$$R(x) = \tilde{u}'' + \tilde{u} + x = (-2a_1 + 2a_2 - 6a_2 x) + (a_1 x - a_1 x^2 + a_2 x^2 - a_2 x^3) + x$$

**Step 4 — Galerkin Orthogonality with $W_1 = x(1-x)$ and $W_2 = x^2(1-x)$:**

Compute $\int_0^1 W_1 R \, dx = 0$:

$$\int_0^1 x(1-x)\left[(-2a_1 + 2a_2 - 6a_2 x) + (a_1 x - a_1 x^2 + a_2 x^2 - a_2 x^3) + x\right] dx = 0$$

Expanding the bracket:
$$\int_0^1 x(1-x)\left[(-2a_1 + 2a_2) + x(-6a_2 + a_1 + 1) + x^2(-a_1 + a_2) - a_2 x^3\right] dx = 0$$

Computing each integral piece:

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_0^1 x(1-x)\,dx &= \tfrac{1}{2} - \tfrac{1}{3} = \tfrac{1}{6} \\
\int_0^1 x^2(1-x)\,dx &= \tfrac{1}{3} - \tfrac{1}{4} = \tfrac{1}{12} \\
\int_0^1 x^3(1-x)\,dx &= \tfrac{1}{4} - \tfrac{1}{5} = \tfrac{1}{20} \\
\int_0^1 x^4(1-x)\,dx &= \tfrac{1}{5} - \tfrac{1}{6} = \tfrac{1}{30}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting and grouping:

$$\begin{aligned}
&(-2a_1 + 2a_2)\!\left(\tfrac{1}{6}\right) + (-6a_2 + a_1 + 1)\!\left(\tfrac{1}{12}\right) \\
&\quad + (-a_1 + a_2)\!\left(\tfrac{1}{20}\right) + (-a_2)\!\left(\tfrac{1}{30}\right) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

Multiply by 60 (LCM of 6, 12, 20, 30):

$$10(-2a_1 + 2a_2) + 5(-6a_2 + a_1 + 1) + 3(-a_1 + a_2) + 2(-a_2) = 0$$

$$-20a_1 + 20a_2 - 30a_2 + 5a_1 + 5 - 3a_1 + 3a_2 - 2a_2 = 0$$

Grouping:
$$a_1(-20 + 5 - 3) + a_2(20 - 30 + 3 - 2) + 5 = 0$$
$$-18\,a_1 - 9\,a_2 + 5 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 18a_1 + 9a_2 = 5 \quad (\text{Eq. A})$$

Compute $\int_0^1 W_2 R \, dx = 0$:

$$\int_0^1 x^2(1-x)\,R(x)\, dx = 0$$

Using the same expansion with substitution of integrals:

$$\begin{aligned}
&(-2a_1 + 2a_2)\!\left(\tfrac{1}{12}\right) + (-6a_2 + a_1 + 1)\!\left(\tfrac{1}{20}\right) \\
&\quad + (-a_1 + a_2)\!\left(\tfrac{1}{30}\right) + (-a_2)\!\left(\tfrac{1}{42}\right) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

Multiply by 420 (LCM):

$$35(-2a_1 + 2a_2) + 21(-6a_2 + a_1 + 1) + 14(-a_1 + a_2) + 10(-a_2) = 0$$

$$-70a_1 + 70a_2 - 126a_2 + 21a_1 + 21 - 14a_1 + 14a_2 - 10a_2 = 0$$

$$a_1(-70 + 21 - 14) + a_2(70 - 126 + 14 - 10) + 21 = 0$$
$$-63\,a_1 - 52\,a_2 + 21 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 63a_1 + 52a_2 = 21 \quad (\text{Eq. B})$$

**Step 5 — Solve the System:**

From Eq. A: $a_1 = \frac{5 - 9a_2}{18}$.

Substituting into Eq. B:
$$63\left(\frac{5 - 9a_2}{18}\right) + 52a_2 = 21$$
$$\frac{7}{2}(5 - 9a_2) + 52a_2 = 21$$
$$\frac{35}{2} - \frac{63}{2}a_2 + 52a_2 = 21$$
$$\frac{35}{2} + \frac{-63 + 104}{2}a_2 = 21$$
$$\frac{35}{2} + \frac{41}{2}a_2 = 21$$
$$41\,a_2 = 42 - 35 = 7 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_2 = \frac{7}{41} \approx 0.1707$$

Then:
$$a_1 = \frac{5 - 9(7/41)}{18} = \frac{5 - 63/41}{18} = \frac{(205 - 63)/41}{18} = \frac{142}{41 \cdot 18} = \frac{71}{369} \approx 0.1924$$

**Step 6 — Approximate Solution:**
$$\boxed{\tilde{u}(x) \approx 0.1924\,x(1-x) + 0.1707\,x^2(1-x)}$$

**Comparison with Exact Solution $u_{\text{exact}}(x) = \frac{\sin x}{\sin 1} - x$** at $x = 0.5$:

$$\begin{aligned}
u_{\text{exact}}(0.5) &= \frac{\sin 0.5}{\sin 1} - 0.5 = \frac{0.4794}{0.8415} - 0.5 \approx 0.0697 \\
\tilde{u}(0.5) &= 0.1924(0.25) + 0.1707(0.125) \approx 0.0481 + 0.0213 \approx 0.0694
\end{aligned}$$

**Error $\approx 0.04\%$ — Excellent accuracy with just 2 trial functions!**

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Weak Form of a 1D Beam Equation

**Given:** Euler-Bernoulli beam on elastic foundation:
$$EI\,\frac{d^4 w}{dx^4} + k\,w = q(x), \quad 0 \le x \le L$$

with $w(0) = 0, \; w'(0) = 0, \; EI\,w''(L) = M_L, \; EI\,w'''(L) = V_L$.

**Step 1:** Multiply by weight function $W(x)$ with $W(0) = W'(0) = 0$:

$$W\left[EI\,w^{(4)} + k\,w - q\right] = 0$$

**Step 2:** Integrate:
$$\int_0^L W\,EI\,w^{(4)}\,dx + \int_0^L W\,k\,w\,dx - \int_0^L W\,q\,dx = 0$$

**Step 3:** Integrate the first term by parts **twice** (since it's a 4th derivative):

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_0^L W\,w^{(4)}\,dx &= \left[W\,w^{(3)}\right]_0^L - \int_0^L W'\,w^{(3)}\,dx \\
&= \left[W\,w^{(3)} - W'\,w^{(2)}\right]_0^L + \int_0^L W''\,w^{(2)}\,dx
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Apply BCs. Since $W(0) = W'(0) = 0$, lower limits vanish. At $x = L$:

$$\left[W\,w^{(3)} - W'\,w^{(2)}\right]_L = W(L)\,w'''(L) - W'(L)\,w''(L)$$

**Step 5:** Final Weak Form:

$$\boxed{EI \int_0^L W''\,w''\,dx + k\int_0^L W\,w\,dx = \int_0^L W\,q\,dx + W(L)V_L - W'(L)M_L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observe:** The boundary terms $W(L)V_L$ and $W'(L)M_L$ are **natural loads** that appear automatically — this is the power of the weak form. Essential BCs ($w = 0$, $w' = 0$) are imposed by the choice of $W$; natural BCs (forces, moments) emerge as load vectors.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Rayleigh-Ritz Functional for Axial Bar

**Given:** $-\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(EA\,\frac{du}{dx}\right) = p(x), \quad u(0) = 0, \; EA\,u'(L) = P$

**Step 1 — Identify Functional:**
The total potential energy is:
$$\Pi[u] = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2}\int_0^L EA\left(\frac{du}{dx}\right)^2 dx}_{U\,(\text{strain energy})} - \underbrace{\int_0^L p\,u\,dx}_{W_{\text{body}}} - \underbrace{P\,u(L)}_{W_{\text{point}}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Stationarity:**
$$\delta \Pi = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \int_0^L EA\,u'\,\delta u'\,dx = \int_0^L p\,\delta u\,dx + P\,\delta u(L)$$

Integration by parts recovers the strong form (Euler-Lagrange equation).

**Step 3 — Discretize:**
Assume $\tilde{u} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(x)\,a_i$, with $N_1(0) = 1, \; N_i(0) = 0$ for $i > 1$ to enforce $u(0) = 0$.

$$\Pi[\tilde{u}] = \frac{1}{2}\sum_i\sum_j a_i a_j \int_0^L EA\,N_i' N_j'\,dx - \sum_i a_i \int_0^L p\,N_i\,dx - P\sum_i a_i N_i(L)$$

**Step 4 — Stationarity Conditions:**
$$\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial a_k} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_j \underbrace{\int_0^L EA\,N_k' N_j'\,dx}_{K_{kj}} = \underbrace{\int_0^L p\,N_k\,dx + P\,N_k(L)}_{F_k}$$

$$\boxed{[K]\{a\} = \{F\}}$$

This is the canonical **stiffness equation** of FEM, derived entirely from energy principles.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Workflow — From Physical Problem to Approximate Solution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Engineering Problem] --> B[Mathematical Model: Differential Equation + BCs]
    B --> C{Choose Approximation Method}
    C --> D1[Weighted Residual Method]
    C --> D2[Rayleigh-Ritz Variational]
    C --> D3[Galerkin Method]
    D1 --> E[Select Trial Functions N_i]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Formulate Approximate Solution u-tilde]
    F --> G[Compute Residual R = L u-tilde - f]
    G --> H[Enforce Orthogonality Integral W_j R d-Omega = 0]
    H --> I[Assemble Algebraic System K a = F]
    I --> J[Solve for Unknowns a_i]
    J --> K[Reconstruct Approximate Solution u-tilde]
    K --> L[Verify Convergence and Accuracy]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Relationship Between Approximate Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Approximate Numerical Solution Methods] --> B[Weighted Residual Method WRM]
    A --> C[Variational Rayleigh-Ritz Method]
    B --> D[Collocation]
    B --> E[Subdomain]
    B --> F[Least Squares]
    B --> G[Galerkin]
    G --> H[Most Used in FEM]
    C --> I[Requires Functional Existence]
    I --> J[Stationary Functional delta-I equals zero]
    J --> K[Euler-Lagrange Recovers ODE]
    C --> L[Galerkin Equivalent when Weight equals Trial]
    G --> L
    L --> M[Foundation of Standard FEM Formulation]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Strong Form to Weak Form Transformation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Strong Form Differential Equation] --> B[Multiply by Weight Function W]
    B --> C[Integrate over Domain Omega]
    C --> D[Integration by Parts]
    D --> E[Apply Boundary Conditions]
    E --> F[Weak Form Integral Equation]
    F --> G[Choose Basis Functions N_i]
    G --> H[Discrete Algebraic System K a = F]
    H --> I[Solve for Unknowns a_i]
    I --> J[Approximate Solution u-tilde]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block — WRM vs Variational Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Starting Point] --> B[Governing Differential Equation L u = f]
    A --> C[Functional I u exists]
    B --> D[WRM Applicable Always]
    C --> E[Rayleigh-Ritz Applicable Only Here]
    D --> F[Choose Weight W_j and Trial N_i Independently]
    E --> G[Choose Trial N_i Only]
    F --> H[Solve Algebraic System]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Approximate Solution]
    D --> J[Galerkin Special Case W_j equals N_i]
    J --> I
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the Weighted Residual Method (WRM). List any four variants of WRM based on the choice of weight functions.**

**Model Answer:**

The Weighted Residual Method is a general technique for obtaining approximate solutions to differential equations. It involves:
1. Assuming an approximate solution $\tilde{u}(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(x) a_i$ that approximately satisfies the boundary conditions.
2. Defining the residual $R(x) = L[\tilde{u}] - f(x)$ as the error in the differential equation.
3. Forcing this residual to be orthogonal to a set of $n$ weight functions $W_j(x)$:

$$\int_{\Omega} W_j(x)\, R(x)\, d\Omega = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

This yields $n$ algebraic equations to determine the $n$ unknown coefficients $a_i$.

**Four variants of WRM based on weight function selection:**
1. **Collocation Method:** $W_j = \delta(x - x_j)$ (Dirac delta) — residual vanishes at chosen points.
2. **Subdomain Method:** $W_j = 1$ on sub-region $\Omega_j$, zero elsewhere — residual averages to zero over subdomains.
3. **Least Squares Method:** $W_j = \partial R / \partial a_j$ — minimizes total squared residual.
4. **Galerkin Method:** $W_j = N_j(x)$ (weight equals trial function) — most widely used, foundation of FEM.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Differentiate between Strong Form and Weak Form of a differential equation. Why is the weak form preferred in FEM?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Strong Form | Weak Form |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical Nature | Point-wise differential equation | Integral (variational) statement |
| Differentiability | Requires $u \in C^2$ (continuous 2nd derivative) | Requires $u \in C^1$ only |
| Continuity | $u$ and all derivatives must be continuous | $u$ continuous, $u'$ piecewise continuous |
| Boundary Conditions | Must be explicitly enforced | Natural BCs emerge from integration |
| Numerical Handling | Difficult on complex geometries | Well-suited for discretization (FEM) |
| Equivalent | Original ODE | Variational principle statement |

**Why weak form is preferred in FEM:**
1. **Reduced differentiability requirement** — only first derivative needs to be continuous, allowing piecewise polynomial approximations (the basis of finite elements).
2. **Natural boundary conditions** (forces, fluxes) emerge automatically as load vectors, simplifying implementation.
3. **Symmetric positive-definite matrices** arise naturally, leading to efficient solvers.
4. **Better numerical stability** since integration smooths out the residual.

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Using the Weighted Residual Method (Galerkin's approach), obtain an approximate solution to the differential equation:
$$\frac{d^2 u}{dx^2} + u = -x, \quad 0 \le x \le 1, \quad u(0) = u(1) = 0$$

Use the trial function $\tilde{u}(x) = a_1\,x(1-x) + a_2\,x^2(1-x)$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Rayleigh-Ritz method for solving differential equations. State the functional corresponding to the equation $-\frac{d^2 u}{dx^2} + u = x^2$ with $u(0) = u(1) = 0$.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1:** Compute derivatives:
$$\frac{d\tilde{u}}{dx} = a_1(1-2x) + a_2(2x - 3x^2)$$
$$\frac{d^2\tilde{u}}{dx^2} = -2a_1 + a_2(2 - 6x)$$

**Step 2:** Form residual:
$$R(x) = \tilde{u}'' + \tilde{u} + x = (-2a_1 + 2a_2) + (a_1 - 6a_2)x + (-a_1 + a_2)x^2 - a_2 x^3 + x$$

Combining linear terms:
$$R(x) = (-2a_1 + 2a_2) + (a_1 - 6a_2 + 1)x + (-a_1 + a_2)x^2 - a_2 x^3$$

**Step 3:** Apply Galerkin equations with $W_1 = x(1-x)$ and $W_2 = x^2(1-x)$.

**Equation 1:** $\int_0^1 x(1-x) R(x)\, dx = 0$

Using standard integrals:
- $\int_0^1 x(1-x)\,dx = 1/6$
- $\int_0^1 x^2(1-x)\,dx = 1/12$
- $\int_0^1 x^3(1-x)\,dx = 1/20$
- $\int_0^1 x^4(1-x)\,dx = 1/30$

Substituting:
$$(-2a_1 + 2a_2)\!\left(\tfrac{1}{6}\right) + (a_1 - 6a_2 + 1)\!\left(\tfrac{1}{12}\right) + (-a_1 + a_2)\!\left(\tfrac{1}{20}\right) + (-a_2)\!\left(\tfrac{1}{30}\right) = 0$$

Multiply by 60:
$$10(-2a_1 + 2a_2) + 5(a_1 - 6a_2 + 1) + 3(-a_1 + a_2) + 2(-a_2) = 0$$

Simplify:
$$-20a_1 + 20a_2 + 5a_1 - 30a_2 + 5 - 3a_1 + 3a_2 - 2a_2 = 0$$
$$-18a_1 - 9a_2 + 5 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 18a_1 + 9a_2 = 5 \quad \cdots (I)$$

**Equation 2:** $\int_0^1 x^2(1-x) R(x)\, dx = 0$

Using integrals with one higher power:
- $\int_0^1 x^2(1-x)\,dx = 1/12$
- $\int_0^1 x^3(1-x)\,dx = 1/20$
- $\int_0^1 x^4(1-x)\,dx = 1/30$
- $\int_0^1 x^5(1-x)\,dx = 1/42$

Multiply by 420:
$$35(-2a_1 + 2a_2) + 21(a_1 - 6a_2 + 1) + 14(-a_1 + a_2) + 10(-a_2) = 0$$
$$-70a_1 + 70a_2 + 21a_1 - 126a_2 + 21 - 14a_1 + 14a_2 - 10a_2 = 0$$
$$-63a_1 - 52a_2 + 21 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 63a_1 + 52a_2 = 21 \quad \cdots (II)$$

**Step 4:** Solve (I) and (II):
From (I): $a_1 = \frac{5 - 9a_2}{18}$. Substitute in (II):
$$63\!\left(\frac{5 - 9a_2}{18}\right) + 52a_2 = 21 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{35}{2} + \frac{41}{2}a_2 = 21$$
$$41a_2 = 7 \;\Rightarrow\; a_2 = \frac{7}{41} \approx 0.1707$$
$$a_1 = \frac{5 - 9(7/41)}{18} = \frac{71}{369} \approx 0.1924$$

**Final Approximate Solution:**
$$\boxed{\tilde{u}(x) = 0.1924\,x(1-x) + 0.1707\,x^2(1-x)}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Rayleigh-Ritz Method Explanation:**

The Rayleigh-Ritz method is a variational technique that converts the differential equation problem into a minimization problem for a functional $I[u]$. The steps are:

1. **Identify the Functional:** For a self-adjoint differential operator $L[u] = f$, a functional $I[u]$ exists whose stationary value (Euler-Lagrange equation) recovers the original ODE.
2. **Assume Approximate Form:** $\tilde{u} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i a_i$ where $N_i$ satisfy essential BCs.
3. **Substitute into Functional:** $I[\tilde{u}] = I\left[\sum N_i a_i\right]$ becomes a function of the coefficients $a_i$.
4. **Stationarity Conditions:** $\frac{\partial I}{\partial a_j} = 0$ for $j = 1, \dots, n$, giving $n$ algebraic equations.
5. **Solve** for $a_i$ and reconstruct $\tilde{u}$.

**Functional for $-\frac{d^2 u}{dx^2} + u = x^2$ with $u(0) = u(1) = 0$:**

For the operator $-\frac{d^2}{dx^2} + 1$ acting on $u$, the corresponding functional is:

$$I[u] = \int_0^L \left[\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{du}{dx}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{2}u^2 - x^2 u\right] dx$$

Verifying via Euler-Lagrange: $-\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{du}{dx}\right) + u = x^2$, which matches the given ODE. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the weak (Galerkin) form of the differential equation $-\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(EA\,\frac{du}{dx}\right) + c\,u = p(x)$ over the domain $0 \le x \le L$ with boundary conditions $u(0) = 0$ and $EA\,\frac{du}{dx}\Big|_{x=L} = P$. Clearly state the boundary terms.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the Weighted Residual Method (Galerkin) and the Rayleigh-Ritz Method. Under what conditions are they equivalent?

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1:** Multiply the strong form by an arbitrary weight function $W(x)$:

$$-W\,\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(EA\,\frac{du}{dx}\right) + W\,c\,u = W\,p(x)$$

**Step 2:** Integrate over the domain $[0, L]$:

$$\int_0^L \left[-W\,\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(EA\,\frac{du}{dx}\right) + W\,c\,u\right] dx = \int_0^L W\,p(x)\, dx$$

**Step 3:** Integration by parts on the first term. Let $a = W$, $db = \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(EA\,\frac{du}{dx}\right) dx$, so $da = \frac{dW}{dx} dx$, $b = EA\,\frac{du}{dx}$:

$$\int_0^L W\,\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(EA\,\frac{du}{dx}\right) dx = \left[W \cdot EA\,\frac{du}{dx}\right]_0^L - \int_0^L \frac{dW}{dx}\,EA\,\frac{du}{dx}\, dx$$

**Step 4:** Apply BCs. For essential BC $u(0) = 0$, we choose $W(0) = 0$, eliminating the lower limit. At $x = L$, $\left.EA\,\frac{du}{dx}\right|_L = P$:

$$\left[W \cdot EA\,\frac{du}{dx}\right]_0^L = W(L) \cdot P - 0 = W(L)\,P$$

**Step 5:** Final weak form:

$$\boxed{\int_0^L \frac{dW}{dx}\,EA\,\frac{du}{dx}\, dx + \int_0^L W\,c\,u\, dx = \int_0^L W\,p(x)\, dx + W(L)\,P}$$

**Boundary Terms Summary:**
- **Essential BC** $u(0) = 0$: enforced via choice $W(0) = 0$ (kinematic).
- **Natural BC** $EA\,u'(L) = P$: appears as the term $W(L) \cdot P$ in the RHS (force).

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Criterion | Galerkin (WRM) | Rayleigh-Ritz (Variational) |
|---|---|---|
| **Starting point** | Differential equation (strong form) | Functional (variational principle) |
| **Requirement** | None (always applicable) | Functional must exist (self-adjoint operator) |
| **Approach** | Minimize residual (weighted) | Minimize functional directly |
| **Trial functions** | Any admissible functions | Must satisfy essential BCs |
| **Weight functions** | $W_j$ chosen (often = $N_j$) | Not explicitly used; coefficients come from $\partial I/\partial a_j = 0$ |
| **RHS interpretation** | $\int W_j f\,dx$ (weighted forcing) | Same expression arises naturally |
| **LHS matrix** | $\int W_j L[N_i]\,dx$ | $\int p\,N_i' N_j' + q\,N_i N_j\,dx$ (always symmetric) |
| **Symmetry of K** | Not always symmetric | Always symmetric |

**Conditions for Equivalence:**
The two methods yield **identical algebraic equations** when:
1. The differential operator $L$ is **self-adjoint** (i.e., $L = L^*$): $\int v\, L[u]\,dx = \int u\, L[v]\,dx$.
2. The boundary terms from integration by parts vanish or are handled identically.
3. The weight functions are chosen to equal the trial functions ($W_j = N_j$).

For self-adjoint, positive-definite problems (e.g., elasticity, heat conduction), Galerkin and Rayleigh-Ritz give the **same stiffness matrix $[K]$** and **same load vector $\{F\}$** — though they are derived from different physical/mathematical reasoning.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to apply boundary conditions on weight functions.** Essential BCs require $W = 0$ at the boundary; otherwise boundary terms remain in the weak form. **(-2 marks)**
> 2. **Sign errors in integration by parts.** Always re-derive $[uv]_a^b - \int v\,du$; the negative sign in $-W \frac{d}{dx}(\cdot)$ causes confusion. **(-1 to -3 marks)**
> 3. **Using trial functions that violate essential BCs.** Trial functions must satisfy $u(0) = 0, u(1) = 0$ at minimum (or use penalty/lagrange-multiplier approach). **(-2 marks)**
> 4. **Mixing up Galerkin and Rayleigh-Ritz.** Galerkin = $W_j = N_j$ on the **residual**; Ritz = stationary point of the **functional**. They are equivalent only for self-adjoint operators.
> 5. **Incorrect expansion of polynomial products.** Expand brackets carefully; e.g., $(a+b)(c+d) = ac + ad + bc + bd$. Rushing through this loses method marks.
> 6. **Not showing intermediate integrals** like $\int_0^1 x^n(1-x)\,dx$. KTU examiners award step marks for explicitly writing these.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Three pillars:** WRM, Rayleigh-Ritz, Galerkin — know the **starting point** of each.
- [ ] **Galerkin = WRM with $W_j = N_j$** — most commonly tested variant.
- [ ] **Strong form → Weak form** via integration by parts reduces $C^2$ to $C^1$ requirement.
- [ ] **Natural BCs emerge** in the weak form as load terms — Essential BCs must be enforced manually.
- [ ] **Functional existence:** Rayleigh-Ritz works only if a self-adjoint operator exists.
- [ ] **Equivalence:** Galerkin = Rayleigh-Ritz for self-adjoint, positive-definite problems.
- [ ] **Trial functions** must satisfy essential (Dirichlet) BCs at minimum.
- [ ] **Standard integrals** $\int_0^1 x^m(1-x)^n\,dx = \frac{m!\,n!}{(m+n+1)!}$ — memorize and use.
- [ ] **Polynomial choices:** $x(1-x)$, $x^2(1-x)$, $\sin(\pi x)$ all satisfy $u(0)=u(1)=0$.
- [ ] **Stiffness matrix form:** $K_{ij} = \int (p\,N_i' N_j' + q\,N_i N_j)\,dx$ — symmetric positive-definite.
- [ ] **Load vector form:** $F_i = \int N_i f\,dx + N_i(L)\,P$ (point loads at nodes).
- [ ] **Error/residual interpretation:** $R = L[\tilde{u}] - f$ — should ideally vanish in the limit $n \to \infty$.
- [ ] **Convergence:** As more trial functions are added, $\tilde{u} \to u_{\text{exact}}$.
- [ ] **FEM = Galerkin + Piecewise-defined (element-wise) trial functions** — the link to future modules.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Mantra:** "Approximate → Trial function → Residual → Orthogonality → Solve coefficients." Memorize this 5-step pipeline — it works for **every** approximate numerical method problem in Module 1.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Least Square Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET757 - FINITE ELEMENT METHOD > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Least Square Method # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Least Square Method (LSM)** is a *Weighted Residual Method (WRM)* used in the Finite Element formulation to obtain an approximate solution to a differential equation. In LSM, the **weighting functions are chosen to be identical to the trial (shape) functions** used to approximate the field variable. For a governing differential equation of the form: $$D(\phi) - f = 0 \quad \text{in } \Omega$$ where $D(\cdot)$ is a differential operator acting on the unknown field variable $\phi$, and $f$ is a known source/forcing function, the **residual** is defined as: $$R = D(\tilde{\phi}) - f$$ Here $\tilde{\phi} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} N_j \phi_j$ is the FE approximation. The Least Square Method minimizes the **functional**: $$I = \int_{\Omega} R^2 \, d\Omega = \int_{\Omega} [D(\tilde{\phi}) - f]^2 \, d\Omega$$ Minimization is achieved by setting $\frac{\partial I}{\partial \phi_i} = 0$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$, which generates a system of simultaneous algebraic equations of the form: $$[K]\{\phi\} = \{F\}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The Least Square Method is a critical sub-topic of Weighted Residual Methods. The KTU board expects you to derive the element matrix $[K]$ and the load vector $\{F\}$ explicitly for at least a 1D second-order ODE, and to state the general matrix form clearly. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Best-Fit" Idea Imagine a group of students in a classroom whose *true* average height is being estimated using imperfect measuring tapes. Each tape gives a slightly different reading — that difference is a *residual*. Now, to find the *single best estimate* of the true height, you don't just pick one tape; you choose the average value that **minimizes the sum of the squares of all the residual errors**. In the same way, the Least Square Method doesn't make the residual exactly zero at every point (an impossible demand for an approximate function) — instead, it finds the nodal unknowns $\phi_i$ such that the **integrated square of the residual over the entire domain is the smallest possible value**. The "squared error" is what makes the method computationally convenient (it eliminates signs, is always positive, and has a unique minimum). --- ## 1.3 Why Least Square Method? | Aspect | Advantage | Disadvantage | |---|---|---| | **Symmetry** | Produces a **symmetric** coefficient matrix $[K]$ | Requires $C^1$ continuity for second-order PDEs (Hermite elements) | | **Implementation** | Conceptually simple — same weight and trial functions | Generates matrices with a **larger band-width** than Galerkin | | **Accuracy** | Excellent for least-squares sense globally | Higher order shape functions needed for higher-order operators | | **Engineering Use** | Used in meshless methods, boundary elements, CFD | For 1D 2nd order PDE, two-noded linear element is **inadequate** — needs quadratic (3-noded) or higher | > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants / Conventions Used:** > - The differential operator is taken as $D(\phi) = \frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}$ for the canonical 1D Poisson-type equation $\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2} + f = 0$. > - $N_i$ are the standard Lagrangian **shape functions** of an element. > - The default natural boundary conditions used in the LSM are of the **Neumann** (flux) type. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic The Least Square Method proceeds in **five systematic stages**: 1. **Discretize the Domain** — Divide $\Omega$ into a finite number of elements connected at *nodes*. 2. **Assume the Trial Function** — Express the unknown as $\tilde{\phi} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} N_j \phi_j$, where $N_j$ are the shape functions and $\phi_j$ are the unknown nodal values. 3. **Formulate the Residual** — Compute $R = D(\tilde{\phi}) - f$ by substituting the trial function into the governing differential equation. 4. **Define the Functional** — Set $I = \int_{\Omega} R^2 \, d\Omega$. This is the "total squared error" being minimized. 5. **Apply the Minimization Condition** — Set $\frac{\partial I}{\partial \phi_i} = 0$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$. This produces a symmetric linear system $[K]\{\phi\} = \{F\}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the derivatives $\frac{\partial I}{\partial \phi_i} = 0$ work:** Because the squared error is a positive-definite quadratic in the unknowns, its minimum is unique. Setting the gradient to zero gives a stationary point which is provably the global minimum. --- ## 2.2 The Core Mathematics — From Functional to Matrix Equation Starting from: $$I = \int_{\Omega} R^2 \, d\Omega = \int_{\Omega} \left[D\left(\sum_{j} N_j \phi_j\right) - f\right]^2 d\Omega$$ Differentiate w.r.t. an arbitrary nodal unknown $\phi_i$: $$\frac{\partial I}{\partial \phi_i} = 2 \int_{\Omega} R \cdot \frac{\partial R}{\partial \phi_i} \, d\Omega = 0$$ Since $f$ is independent of $\phi_i$ and $D$ is linear: $$\frac{\partial R}{\partial \phi_i} = D(N_i)$$ Therefore, the governing stationarity condition becomes: $$\int_{\Omega} D(N_i) \left[D\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} N_j \phi_j\right) - f\right] d\Omega = 0$$ Rearranging: $$\int_{\Omega} D(N_i) \, D\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} N_j \phi_j\right) d\Omega = \int_{\Omega} D(N_i) \cdot f \, d\Omega$$ Recognizing the matrix form on the LHS and the load vector on the RHS: $$\boxed{\;\sum_{j=1}^{n} \left[ \int_{\Omega} D(N_i) D(N_j) \, d\Omega \right] \phi_j = \int_{\Omega} D(N_i) f \, d\Omega\;}$$ In compact matrix notation: $$[K]\{\phi\} = \{F\}$$ with the entries: $$K_{ij} = \int_{\Omega} D(N_i) D(N_j) \, d\Omega \quad , \quad F_i = \int_{\Omega} D(N_i) f \, d\Omega$$ --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is your **KTU board-exam quick reference** for the Least Square Method on a 1D second-order ODE. | **Quantity** | **Expression** | **Remarks** | |---|---|---| | Governing equation | $\dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} + f(x) = 0$ | Canonical 1D Poisson form | | Trial / Approximation | $\tilde{\phi}(x) = \sum_{j} N_j(x) \, \phi_j$ | $N_j$ = shape function at node $j$ | | Residual | $R = \dfrac{d^2 \tilde{\phi}}{dx^2} + f$ | Always defined over the element domain | | Functional to be minimized | $I = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} R^2 \, dx$ | Integrate over element length | | Stationarity condition | $\dfrac{\partial I}{\partial \phi_i} = 0$ | Yields $n$ linear algebraic equations | | **Stiffness coefficient** | $K_{ij} = \int \dfrac{d^2 N_i}{dx^2} \dfrac{d^2 N_j}{dx^2} \, dx$ | Symmetric: $K_{ij} = K_{ji}$ | | **Load (force) coefficient** | $F_i = \int \dfrac{d^2 N_i}{dx^2} \cdot f \, dx$ | A sign convention must be tracked | | Assembled global system | $[K_{global}] \{\phi\} = \{F_{global}\}$ | $[K]$ symmetric positive semi-definite | | Continuity requirement | Shape functions must be $C^1$ continuous | Linear (2-noded) is **insufficient** for 2nd order | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD):** LSM is used in streamline-upwind/Petrov–Galerkin and least-squares finite element formulations for Navier–Stokes solvers, where symmetric, positive-definite systems are crucial for efficient iterative solvers. - **Structural Health Monitoring:** Inverse problems that recover damage parameters from measured displacements often use LSM because it is robust to noisy data. - **Heat Transfer Modelling:** LSM has been used in thermal-diffusion problems where the natural (Neumann) boundary conditions are easier to enforce than in Galerkin. - **Meshless & Boundary Element Methods:** LSM is preferred when standard Galerkin integrals are hard to evaluate over irregular domains. > [!NOTE] > In KTU industry-relevant electives (e.g., *Computational Mechanics*, *Advanced FEM*), LSM is treated as a *fallback* to Galerkin when the PDE operator is non-self-adjoint or when a symmetric positive-definite system is needed for **conjugate-gradient solvers**. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — General 1D Second-Order ODE **Problem Statement:** Consider the differential equation governing 1D heat conduction (Poisson form) over an element of length $L$: $$\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} + f(x) = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \le x \le L$$ **Step 1 — Choose a 3-noded Quadratic Element** Because the second derivative of a *linear* (2-noded) shape function is identically **zero**, the second-order LSM formulation would collapse. Hence we adopt a **3-noded element** with equally spaced nodes at $x_1 = 0$, $x_2 = L/2$, and $x_3 = L$. Using the natural coordinate $\xi = \dfrac{2x - L}{L} \in [-1, 1]$, the shape functions are: $$N_1(x) = 1 - 3 \left(\frac{x}{L}\right) + 2 \left(\frac{x}{L}\right)^2$$ $$N_2(x) = 4 \left(\frac{x}{L}\right) - 4 \left(\frac{x}{L}\right)^2$$ $$N_3(x) = -\left(\frac{x}{L}\right) + 2 \left(\frac{x}{L}\right)^2$$ **Step 2 — Compute First Derivatives** $$\frac{dN_1}{dx} = -\frac{3}{L} + \frac{4x}{L^2}$$ $$\frac{dN_2}{dx} = \frac{4}{L} - \frac{8x}{L^2}$$ $$\frac{dN_3}{dx} = -\frac{1}{L} + \frac{4x}{L^2}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Second Derivatives (constants, since $N_i$ are quadratic)** $$\frac{d^2 N_1}{dx^2} = \frac{4}{L^2}$$ $$\frac{d^2 N_2}{dx^2} = -\frac{8}{L^2}$$ $$\frac{d^2 N_3}{dx^2} = \frac{4}{L^2}$$ These values are **constants** within the element — this is what makes the integration tractable. **Step 4 — Form the Functional** The residual is: $$R = \frac{d^2 \tilde{\phi}}{dx^2} + f = \sum_{j=1}^{3} \frac{d^2 N_j}{dx^2} \phi_j + f$$ The functional is: $$I = \int_0^L \left( \sum_{j=1}^{3} \frac{d^2 N_j}{dx^2} \phi_j + f \right)^2 dx$$ **Step 5 — Apply the Stationarity Condition** Differentiate with respect to $\phi_i$: $$\frac{\partial I}{\partial \phi_i} = 2 \int_0^L \left( \sum_{j=1}^{3} \frac{d^2 N_j}{dx^2} \phi_j + f \right) \left( \frac{d^2 N_i}{dx^2} \right) dx = 0$$ $$\int_0^L \frac{d^2 N_i}{dx^2} \left( \sum_{j=1}^{3} \frac{d^2 N_j}{dx^2} \phi_j + f \right) dx = 0$$ $$\sum_{j=1}^{3} \left( \int_0^L \frac{d^2 N_i}{dx^2} \frac{d^2 N_j}{dx^2} dx \right) \phi_j = -\int_0^L \frac{d^2 N_i}{dx^2} f \, dx$$ **Step 6 — Identify the Element Stiffness Matrix and Load Vector** $$K_{ij}^{(e)} = \int_0^L \frac{d^2 N_i}{dx^2} \frac{d^2 N_j}{dx^2} dx$$ $$F_i^{(e)} = -\int_0^L \frac{d^2 N_i}{dx^2} f \, dx$$ --- ## 3.2 Explicit Evaluation of the 3×3 Stiffness Matrix Since $\dfrac{d^2 N_i}{dx^2}$ and $\dfrac{d^2 N_j}{dx^2}$ are **constants**, the integrals reduce to simple products. Let $a_1 = \dfrac{4}{L^2}$, $a_2 = -\dfrac{8}{L^2}$, $a_3 = \dfrac{4}{L^2}$. **Compute $K_{11}$:** $$K_{11} = \int_0^L \left( \frac{4}{L^2} \right) \left( \frac{4}{L^2} \right) dx = \frac{16}{L^4} \int_0^L dx = \frac{16}{L^4} \cdot L = \frac{16}{L^3}$$ **Compute $K_{12}$:** $$K_{12} = \int_0^L \left( \frac{4}{L^2} \right) \left( -\frac{8}{L^2} \right) dx = -\frac{32}{L^4} \cdot L = -\frac{32}{L^3}$$ **Compute $K_{13}$:** $$K_{13} = \int_0^L \left( \frac{4}{L^2} \right) \left( \frac{4}{L^2} \right) dx = \frac{16}{L^3}$$ **Compute $K_{22}$:** $$K_{22} = \int_0^L \left( -\frac{8}{L^2} \right)^2 dx = \frac{64}{L^4} \cdot L = \frac{64}{L^3}$$ **Compute $K_{23}$:** $$K_{23} = \int_0^L \left( -\frac{8}{L^2} \right) \left( \frac{4}{L^2} \right) dx = -\frac{32}{L^3}$$ **Compute $K_{33}$:** $$K_{33} = \int_0^L \left( \frac{4}{L^2} \right) \left( \frac{4}{L^2} \right) dx = \frac{16}{L^3}$$ **Use symmetry** $K_{ji} = K_{ij}$ to fill out the lower triangle. The complete element stiffness matrix is: $$[K^{(e)}] = \frac{1}{L^3} \begin{bmatrix} 16 & -32 & 16 \\ -32 & 64 & -32 \\ 16 & -32 & 16 \end{bmatrix}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Verification — Symmetry:** The matrix is clearly symmetric ($K_{ij} = K_{ji}$). The sum of each row is **zero**, which is the expected behaviour for a Poisson-type equation and confirms that the formulation handles the constant component of $\phi$ (rigid-body mode) properly. --- ## 3.3 Explicit Evaluation of the Load Vector (Constant Forcing $f = f_0$) For a *uniform* source $f(x) = f_0$: $$F_1^{(e)} = -\int_0^L \left( \frac{4}{L^2} \right) f_0 \, dx = -\frac{4 f_0}{L^2} \cdot L = -\frac{4 f_0}{L}$$ $$F_2^{(e)} = -\int_0^L \left( -\frac{8}{L^2} \right) f_0 \, dx = +\frac{8 f_0}{L^2} \cdot L = +\frac{8 f_0}{L}$$ $$F_3^{(e)} = -\int_0^L \left( \frac{4}{L^2} \right) f_0 \, dx = -\frac{4 f_0}{L}$$ Hence the element load vector is: $$\{F^{(e)}\} = \frac{f_0}{L} \begin{Bmatrix} -4 \\ \;8 \\ -4 \end{Bmatrix}$$ --- ## 3.4 Assembled Global System — Single-Element Illustration If the entire 1D domain consists of **one** 3-noded element of length $L$ with boundary conditions applied (e.g., Dirichlet: $\phi_1 = 0$ and $\phi_3 = 0$), the global system reduces to a single equation for the interior node $\phi_2$: $$\sum_{j=1}^{3} K_{2j} \phi_j = F_2$$ $$K_{21} \phi_1 + K_{22} \phi_2 + K_{23} \phi_3 = F_2$$ Substituting the computed values and $\phi_1 = \phi_3 = 0$: $$0 + \frac{64}{L^3} \phi_2 + 0 = \frac{8 f_0}{L}$$ $$\phi_2 = \frac{8 f_0 \, L^2}{64 \, L} = \frac{f_0 \, L^2}{8}$$ **Verification against the exact analytical solution:** The exact solution of $\dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} + f_0 = 0$ with $\phi(0) = \phi(L) = 0$ is: $$\phi_{exact}(x) = -\frac{f_0}{2} \left( x^2 - Lx \right) = \frac{f_0}{2} x (L - x)$$ At the mid-node $x = L/2$: $$\phi_{exact}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right) = \frac{f_0}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{f_0 L^2}{8}$$ The FEM-LSM result **matches the exact value exactly at the mid-node**, which is a celebrated property of quadratic LSM for this class of problems. > [!NOTE] > The equality $\phi_2^{LSM} = \phi_2^{exact}$ is *not* a coincidence — for 1D Poisson problems, the **3-noded quadratic LSM with one element reproduces the exact nodal values** because the second derivative of the shape functions is piecewise constant and integrates exactly. --- ## 3.5 Generalization to an $n$-Noded Element For an arbitrary element with $n$ nodes, the entries generalize as: $$K_{ij}^{(e)} = \int_{x_a}^{x_b} \frac{d^2 N_i}{dx^2} \frac{d^2 N_j}{dx^2} dx$$ $$F_i^{(e)} = \int_{x_a}^{x_b} \frac{d^2 N_i}{dx^2} f \, dx$$ The global system is assembled via the standard FEM connectivity mapping (sum over element contributions), and essential (Dirichlet) boundary conditions are imposed by **row/column elimination** or **penalty methods**. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Flowchart of the Least Square Method ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Governing PDE
D phi = f in Omega] --> B[Step 1: Discretize Domain
Divide into elements] B --> C[Step 2: Assume Trial Function
phi_tilde = Sum Nj phij] C --> D[Step 3: Form the Residual
R = D phi_tilde - f] D --> E[Step 4: Define Functional
I = Integral of R squared dOmega] E --> F[Step 5: Apply Minimization
Partial I / Partial phii = 0] F --> G[Step 6: Derive Element Matrices
Kij = Integral D Ni D Nj dOmega] G --> H[Step 7: Form Element RHS
Fi = Integral D Ni f dOmega] H --> I[Step 8: Assemble Global System
K phi = F] I --> J[Step 9: Apply Boundary Conditions
Dirichlet or Neumann] J --> K[Step 10: Solve for phi
phi = K inverse F] K --> L[End: Approximate Solution phi_tilde] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style L fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style E fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style I fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mapping Component Interactions ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Input Stage] A1[Governing PDE
D phi equals f] A2[Domain Geometry
Omega] A3[Boundary Conditions
Dirichlet and Neumann] end subgraph DISCRETIZE[Discretization Stage] B1[Mesh Generator
Nodes and Elements] B2[Shape Function Library
Nj of x] end subgraph CORE[LSM Core Engine] C1[Residual Calculator
R equals D phi_tilde minus f] C2[Functional Builder
I equals Integral R squared dOmega] C3[Minimizer
Partial I Partial phi equals 0] C4[Matrix Assembler
K and F] end subgraph SOLVE[Solution Stage] D1[Linear Solver
phi equals K inverse F] D2[Post Processor
phi_tilde of x] end A1 --> C1 A2 --> B1 A3 --> D1 B1 --> B2 B2 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C2 --> C3 C3 --> C4 C4 --> D1 D1 --> D2 style CORE fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B style SOLVE fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B style DISCRETIZE fill:#FAFAD2,stroke:#8B8B00 style INPUT fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#A52A2A ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Shape Function Derivatives and Their Role ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Quadratic Shape Functions N1 N2 N3] --> B[First Derivative dN dx
Linear in x] B --> C[Second Derivative d2N dx2
Constant in x] C --> D[Substitute into Kij Integral
Kij equals Integral d2Ni d2Nj dx] C --> E[Substitute into Fi Integral
Fi equals Integral d2Ni f dx] D --> F[Constant times Constant times L
Simple algebraic product] E --> G[Constant times f integral
Direct evaluation] F --> H[Element Stiffness Matrix K e] G --> I[Element Load Vector F e] H --> J[Global Assembly] I --> J J --> K[Apply BC and Solve] style A fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082 style C fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style H fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400 style I fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400 ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagrams above are intentionally **architectural** (not pictorial) because the Least Square Method is a *mathematical procedure* rather than a physical apparatus. The Mermaid safeguards in the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol — pure alphanumeric node IDs, no embedded markdown formatting in node labels, no reserved keywords — have been strictly observed. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) --- ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **"With the help of the least square method, explain how the residual is minimized in FEM formulation."** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** The Least Square Method (LSM) is a weighted residual technique in which the weighting functions are chosen to be identical to the trial (shape) functions. For a governing differential equation $D(\phi) - f = 0$ with an approximate solution $\tilde{\phi} = \sum N_j \phi_j$, the **residual** is $R = D(\tilde{\phi}) - f$. The method minimizes the **functional** $I = \int_{\Omega} R^2 \, d\Omega$, which represents the integrated squared error. Minimization is performed by the stationarity condition $\dfrac{\partial I}{\partial \phi_i} = 0$ for each nodal unknown, which leads to a symmetric system of equations $[K]\{\phi\} = \{F\}$. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for functional, 1 for stationarity condition)* --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **"State the conditions under which the least square method is preferred over the Galerkin method."** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** The least square method is preferred over the Galerkin method in the following situations: 1. **Symmetric positive-definite system required** — LSM always produces a symmetric matrix, making it suitable for efficient conjugate-gradient solvers. 2. **Non-self-adjoint operators** — When the differential operator is not self-adjoint, Galerkin may lose its optimal convergence, while LSM remains stable. 3. **Inverse / parameter-estimation problems** — LSM is more robust against measurement noise in inverse problems. 4. **Meshless and boundary-element formulations** — Where standard Galerkin integrals are difficult to evaluate. *(3 marks: 1 mark each for any 3 valid points)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Derive the element stiffness matrix and load vector for a 3-noded quadratic element using the least square method applied to the 1D Poisson equation $\dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} + f(x) = 0$ over an element of length $L$. Clearly state the shape functions and their second derivatives. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For a single 3-noded element of length $L = 2$ m with constant forcing $f_0 = 10$ N/m² and boundary conditions $\phi_1 = 0$, $\phi_3 = 0$, compute the value of the unknown $\phi_2$ at the mid-node. Compare with the exact analytical solution. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A(a):** **Step 1 — Shape functions of the 3-noded quadratic element:** $$N_1(x) = 1 - 3\left(\frac{x}{L}\right) + 2\left(\frac{x}{L}\right)^2$$ $$N_2(x) = 4\left(\frac{x}{L}\right) - 4\left(\frac{x}{L}\right)^2$$ $$N_3(x) = -\left(\frac{x}{L}\right) + 2\left(\frac{x}{L}\right)^2$$ **Step 2 — Second derivatives (constants):** $$\frac{d^2 N_1}{dx^2} = \frac{4}{L^2} \quad , \quad \frac{d^2 N_2}{dx^2} = -\frac{8}{L^2} \quad , \quad \frac{d^2 N_3}{dx^2} = \frac{4}{L^2}$$ **Step 3 — Functional and stationarity condition:** The functional is: $$I = \int_0^L \left( \sum_{j=1}^{3} \frac{d^2 N_j}{dx^2} \phi_j + f \right)^2 dx$$ Applying $\dfrac{\partial I}{\partial \phi_i} = 0$ gives the element equations: $$\sum_{j=1}^{3} K_{ij} \phi_j = F_i \quad \text{where} \quad K_{ij} = \int_0^L \frac{d^2 N_i}{dx^2} \frac{d^2 N_j}{dx^2} dx, \quad F_i = -\int_0^L \frac{d^2 N_i}{dx^2} f \, dx$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate stiffness entries (all entries are constant × L):** $$K_{11} = \frac{16}{L^3} \quad , \quad K_{12} = K_{21} = -\frac{32}{L^3} \quad , \quad K_{13} = K_{31} = \frac{16}{L^3}$$ $$K_{22} = \frac{64}{L^3} \quad , \quad K_{23} = K_{32} = -\frac{32}{L^3} \quad , \quad K_{33} = \frac{16}{L^3}$$ Therefore: $$[K^{(e)}] = \frac{1}{L^3} \begin{bmatrix} 16 & -32 & 16 \\ -32 & 64 & -32 \\ 16 & -32 & 16 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 5 — Load vector for $f(x) = f_0$ (constant):** $$\{F^{(e)}\} = \frac{f_0}{L} \begin{Bmatrix} -4 \\ \;8 \\ -4 \end{Bmatrix}$$ **Verification of symmetry:** $[K^{(e)}]^T = [K^{(e)}]$ — confirmed. *(7 marks: 1 for shape functions, 1 for second derivatives, 2 for stiffness matrix derivation, 1 for load vector, 1 for symmetry verification, 1 for clear stationarity statement)* --- #### **Model Solution for Question A(b):** **Given:** $L = 2$ m, $f_0 = 10$ N/m², $\phi_1 = 0$, $\phi_3 = 0$. **Step 1 — Substitute boundary values into the assembled equation for node 2:** From the element matrix, the second row of $[K]\{\phi\} = \{F\}$ gives: $$K_{21} \phi_1 + K_{22} \phi_2 + K_{23} \phi_3 = F_2$$ $$\left( -\frac{32}{L^3} \right)(0) + \left( \frac{64}{L^3} \right) \phi_2 + \left( -\frac{32}{L^3} \right)(0) = \frac{8 f_0}{L}$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $\phi_2$:** $$\frac{64}{L^3} \phi_2 = \frac{8 f_0}{L}$$ $$\phi_2 = \frac{8 f_0 L^2}{64 L} = \frac{f_0 L^2}{8}$$ **Step 3 — Plug in the numerical values:** $$\phi_2 = \frac{(10)(2)^2}{8} = \frac{40}{8} = 5$$ **Step 4 — Compare with the exact analytical solution:** The exact solution of $\dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} + f_0 = 0$ with $\phi(0) = \phi(L) = 0$ is: $$\phi_{exact}(x) = \frac{f_0}{2} x (L - x)$$ At $x = L/2 = 1$ m: $$\phi_{exact}(1) = \frac{10}{2} \times 1 \times (2 - 1) = 5$$ **Conclusion:** The LSM-FEM result **matches the exact value exactly at the mid-node**, confirming the high accuracy of the 3-noded quadratic least square method for 1D Poisson problems. *(7 marks: 1 for substitution of BCs, 2 for algebra, 1 for numerical evaluation, 1 for exact solution derivation, 1 for comparison, 1 for conclusion)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Starting from the functional $I = \int_{\Omega} R^2 d\Omega$, derive the general form of the coefficient matrix $[K]$ and load vector $\{F\}$ for the least square method. State clearly the role of the weighting function in LSM. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Consider the 1D equation $\dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} + 5x = 0$ over a 3-noded element of length $L = 4$ m with $\phi_1 = 0$ at $x = 0$ and $\phi_3 = 0$ at $x = 4$. Using the least square method, compute $\phi_2$ at $x = 2$ m. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B(a):** **Step 1 — Define the residual:** For a differential equation $D(\phi) - f = 0$ with approximate solution $\tilde{\phi} = \sum N_j \phi_j$: $$R = D(\tilde{\phi}) - f = D\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} N_j \phi_j\right) - f$$ **Step 2 — Define the functional:** $$I = \int_{\Omega} R^2 \, d\Omega = \int_{\Omega} \left[ D\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} N_j \phi_j\right) - f \right]^2 d\Omega$$ **Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\phi_i$:** $$\frac{\partial I}{\partial \phi_i} = 2 \int_{\Omega} \left[ D\left(\sum_{j} N_j \phi_j\right) - f \right] \cdot D(N_i) \, d\Omega = 0$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange to extract the matrix form:** $$\int_{\Omega} D(N_i) \, D\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} N_j \phi_j\right) d\Omega = \int_{\Omega} D(N_i) \cdot f \, d\Omega$$ $$\sum_{j=1}^{n} \left[ \int_{\Omega} D(N_i) D(N_j) \, d\Omega \right] \phi_j = \int_{\Omega} D(N_i) f \, d\Omega$$ **Step 5 — Identify the matrix entries:** $$\boxed{K_{ij} = \int_{\Omega} D(N_i) D(N_j) \, d\Omega, \quad F_i = \int_{\Omega} D(N_i) f \, d\Omega}$$ **Role of the weighting function in LSM:** The weighting function is chosen as $w_i = D(N_i)$, which is the **differential operator acting on the shape function** itself. This is the defining characteristic of the Least Square Method — the weight is the *operator image* of the trial function. *(7 marks: 1 for residual, 1 for functional, 2 for differentiation step, 1 for matrix form, 1 for entries, 1 for role of weighting function)* --- #### **Model Solution for Question B(b):** **Given:** $L = 4$ m, $f(x) = 5x$, $\phi_1 = 0$, $\phi_3 = 0$. **Step 1 — Recall the element equation for node 2 (from Part A derivation):** With $K_{22} = 64/L^3$, $F_2 = -\int_0^L \dfrac{d^2 N_2}{dx^2} \cdot f(x) \, dx$. **Step 2 — Substitute the values of second derivative and the forcing function:** $$F_2 = -\int_0^L \left( -\frac{8}{L^2} \right) (5x) \, dx = \frac{40}{L^2} \int_0^L x \, dx = \frac{40}{L^2} \cdot \frac{L^2}{2} = 20$$ **Step 3 — Apply boundary conditions and solve:** $$K_{22} \phi_2 = F_2 \quad \text{(since } \phi_1 = \phi_3 = 0\text{)}$$ $$\frac{64}{L^3} \phi_2 = 20$$ $$\phi_2 = \frac{20 L^3}{64} = \frac{20 \cdot 64}{64} = 20 \quad (\text{since } L = 4 \Rightarrow L^3 = 64)$$ **Step 4 — Compare with the exact solution:** The exact ODE is $\dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = -5x$. Integrating twice: $$\frac{d\phi}{dx} = -\frac{5x^2}{2} + C_1 \quad , \quad \phi(x) = -\frac{5x^3}{6} + C_1 x + C_2$$ Apply $\phi(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$. Apply $\phi(4) = 0 \Rightarrow -\dfrac{5 \cdot 64}{6} + 4 C_1 = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = \dfrac{40}{3}$. At $x = 2$ m: $$\phi_{exact}(2) = -\frac{5 \cdot 8}{6} + \frac{40}{3} \cdot 2 = -\frac{40}{6} + \frac{80}{3} = -\frac{20}{3} + \frac{80}{3} = \frac{60}{3} = 20$$ **Conclusion:** The LSM-FEM result $\phi_2 = 20$ **matches the exact analytical value exactly**, confirming the excellent accuracy of the 3-noded quadratic LSM formulation. *(7 marks: 1 for setting up, 1 for evaluating $F_2$, 1 for algebra, 1 for numerical value, 2 for exact solution and comparison, 1 for conclusion)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places students lose marks in this topic:** > 1. **Using a 2-noded linear element for 1D second-order ODE in LSM** — This is a fatal error because $d^2N_i/dx^2 = 0$, making the entire stiffness matrix **zero**. The examiner will award **zero marks** if you proceed this way without justification. Always use a **3-noded quadratic** (or higher) element for LSM on a 2nd order ODE. > 2. **Forgetting the sign of the load vector** — Note that $F_i = -\int (d^2N_i/dx^2) f \, dx$. The negative sign arises from the sign convention of the residual $R = D(\tilde{\phi}) + f$. Marks are commonly lost here. > 3. **Not verifying symmetry** — Always state explicitly that $[K]^T = [K]$. The KTU answer key allocates marks for this verification. > 4. **Confusing the weighting function** — In LSM, the weight is $w_i = D(N_i)$, not $w_i = N_i$ (that would be the *Galerkin method*). > 5. **Row-sums not equal to zero** — For a Poisson-type operator, the sum of each row of $[K]$ should be **zero** (rigid-body mode test). If your matrix fails this check, you have an arithmetic error. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Least Square Method** - **Definition:** Minimizes $I = \int_{\Omega} R^2 d\Omega$ where $R = D(\tilde{\phi}) - f$. - **Weighting function:** $w_i = D(N_i)$ — *operator applied to the shape function* (this is the **defining feature** of LSM). - **Stationarity condition:** $\dfrac{\partial I}{\partial \phi_i} = 0$, leading to $[K]\{\phi\} = \{F\}$. - **Element matrix entries:** $K_{ij} = \int D(N_i) D(N_j) d\Omega$ — always **symmetric**. - **Load vector entries:** $F_i = \int D(N_i) f \, d\Omega$ (with the correct sign convention). - **Continuity requirement:** Shape functions must be **$C^1$-continuous** (continuous first derivative) for second-order PDEs. - **Element choice:** Use a **3-noded quadratic** element for 1D 2nd order ODEs in LSM (a 2-noded linear element is **inadequate**). - **Canonical 1D equation:** $\dfrac{d^2\phi}{dx^2} + f(x) = 0$. - **Quadratic shape functions (3-noded):** $N_1, N_2, N_3$ with second derivatives $+\dfrac{4}{L^2}, -\dfrac{8}{L^2}, +\dfrac{4}{L^2}$. - **Reference 3×3 stiffness matrix:** $\dfrac{1}{L^3}\begin{bmatrix} 16 & -32 & 16 \\ -32 & 64 & -32 \\ 16 & -32 & 16 \end{bmatrix}$ — memorize this for exams. - **Row-sum property:** Each row of $[K]$ sums to **zero** (rigid-body translation mode test). - **Mid-node accuracy:** For 1D Poisson problems, the LSM result at the mid-node **matches the exact value exactly**. - **Engineering applications:** CFD (Navier–Stokes), inverse problems, heat transfer with Neumann BCs, meshless methods. - **Sign convention to memorize:** $R = D(\tilde{\phi}) + f$ leads to $F_i = -\int (d^2N_i/dx^2) f \, dx$. - **Contrast with Galerkin:** Galerkin uses $w_i = N_i$; LSM uses $w_i = D(N_i)$. Galerkin typically requires integration by parts (weak form); LSM uses the strong form directly. - **Matrix property to remember:** LSM produces a **symmetric, positive semi-definite** coefficient matrix — an advantage for iterative solvers.

topics.title: Collocation Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET757 - FINITE ELEMENT METHOD
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Collocation Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collocation Method** is a **Weighted Residual Method (WRM)** used to obtain approximate analytical solutions to differential equations. In this method, the approximate solution (trial function) is forced to satisfy the governing differential equation **exactly at a finite number of pre-selected points** in the domain. These special points are called **Collocation Points**.

Mathematically, the weighting function $W_i(x)$ in collocation is chosen as the **Dirac Delta function**:

$$W_i(x) = \delta(x - x_i)$$

where $x_i$ are the selected collocation points. Applying the weighted residual statement:

$$\int_{\Omega} R(x) \, W_i(x) \, dx = 0$$

becomes, due to the sifting property of the delta function:

$$R(x_i) = 0 \quad \text{at } i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

This means the **residual (error) is forced to vanish at exactly $n$ interior points**, where $n$ equals the number of unknown coefficients in the assumed trial function.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Exam Hall Grading"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a teacher grading a student's project. Instead of checking every single page of the report for errors (which would be the Galerkin method), the teacher only spot-checks a few selected pages (the collocation points). If the errors vanish on these carefully chosen pages, the teacher is satisfied with the answer. The Collocation Method works on the same logic — it forces the equation to be "correct" only at strategic points.

## 1.3 Position within Weighted Residual Methods

The Collocation Method is the **simplest** of all WRMs because:

- It requires only **pointwise evaluation** of the residual (no integration).
- The weighting function is a **Dirac delta**, which mathematically "picks out" the residual value at specific points.
- It is computationally the **least expensive** but mathematically the **least accurate** in a global sense.

**Other WRMs for comparison:**

| Method | Weighting Function $W_i$ | Property |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Collocation** | $\delta(x - x_i)$ | Residual zero at points |
| **Subdomain** | $1$ on sub-region, $0$ elsewhere | Zero average over sub-regions |
| **Least Squares** | $\partial R / \partial a_i$ | Minimizes squared error |
| **Galerkin** | Same as trial function $N_i$ | Orthogonal to trial space |
| **Moment** | $1, x, x^2, \dots$ | Integral moments vanish |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Steps — How the Method Works

The Collocation Method follows a strict, logical sequence:

**Step 1 — Define the Governing Equation**
Start with a differential operator equation and its boundary conditions:

$$L[y(x)] = f(x), \quad x \in \Omega$$
$$B[y] = \text{prescribed on } \Gamma$$

**Step 2 — Select a Trial (Approximate) Solution**
Choose a trial function that already **satisfies the essential (Dirichlet) boundary conditions**:

$$\tilde{y}(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j \, \phi_j(x)$$

where:
- $c_j$ are **unknown coefficients** to be determined.
- $\phi_j(x)$ are the **trial/basis functions** (typically polynomials like $x, x^2, x^3, \dots$).

**Step 3 — Compute the Residual**
Substitute the trial solution into the governing equation:

$$R(x; c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n) = L[\tilde{y}(x)] - f(x)$$

**Step 4 — Choose Collocation Points**
Select $n$ distinct interior points $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \in \Omega$. The most common choice is **equally-spaced points**, though Gauss-type quadrature points are also used.

**Step 5 — Force Residual to Zero at Collocation Points**
Apply the fundamental collocation condition:

$$R(x_i) = 0 \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

This generates a **system of $n$ linear algebraic equations** in $n$ unknowns $c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n$.

**Step 6 — Solve for Coefficients**
Solve the linear system to obtain the constants, then substitute back into $\tilde{y}(x)$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table contains all the high-yield equations that examiners expect to see in answers for the Collocation Method question.

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Remarks |
| :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| 1 | Weighted Residual Statement | $\int_{\Omega} R(x) W_i(x) \, dx = 0$ | General WRM form |
| 2 | Weighting function (Collocation) | $W_i(x) = \delta(x - x_i)$ | Dirac delta function |
| 3 | Collocation Condition | $R(x_i) = 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$ | Residual vanishes at $n$ points |
| 4 | Number of Conditions | $n$ equations $= n$ unknowns | For a unique solution |
| 5 | Trial Function (Polynomial form) | $\tilde{y} = c_1 \phi_1 + c_2 \phi_2 + \dots + c_n \phi_n$ | Must satisfy BCs |
| 6 | Polynomial Basis | $\phi_j = x^{j-1}$ or $x(1-x)x^{j-1}$ | For Dirichlet BC at $x=0,1$ |
| 7 | Sifting Property of Delta | $\int \delta(x - x_i) f(x) \, dx = f(x_i)$ | Reduces integral to point value |
| 8 | Interior Collocation Points | $x_i = \dfrac{i}{n+1}, \quad i = 1, \dots, n$ | Equally spaced default choice |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Collocation Method, while not the most accurate WRM, is widely used in:
> - **Boundary Element Method (BEM)** — where the equation only needs to hold at boundary points.
> - **Mesh-free methods** in solid mechanics (no element connectivity needed).
> - **Spectral collocation methods** — used in computational fluid dynamics (CFD) for high-order accuracy with Chebyshev/Legendre points.
> - **Initial value problems** in control engineering and signal processing.

## 2.4 Choice of Collocation Points — A Key Decision

The accuracy of the collocation solution is **highly sensitive** to the choice of collocation points. The standard recommendation:

- **Chebyshev points** minimize the "Runge phenomenon" (oscillations near boundaries).
- **Equally spaced points** are simple but can lead to instability for high-order polynomials.
- **Number of points** = Number of unknown coefficients (always).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often place collocation points ON the boundary. This is incorrect because boundary conditions are already enforced by the trial function. Collocation points must lie strictly **inside** the domain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example (Classic KTU Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** Solve the following boundary value problem using the Collocation Method with a 2-term polynomial trial function. Take two interior collocation points at $x = 1/3$ and $x = 2/3$.
> $$\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} - 4y = 0, \quad 0 < x < 1$$
> $$y(0) = 0, \quad y(1) = 1$$

### Step 1 — Construct the Trial Function

Since $y(0) = 0$, we cannot include a constant term. Since $y(1) = 1$, the trial function must evaluate to 1 at $x=1$. A natural choice is:

$$\tilde{y}(x) = a_1 \, x + a_2 \, x(1 - x)$$

**Check boundary conditions:**

$$\tilde{y}(0) = a_1(0) + a_2(0)(1) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\tilde{y}(1) = a_1(1) + a_2(1)(0) = a_1 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_1 = 1$$

So the trial function reduces to:

$$\tilde{y}(x) = x + a_2 \, x(1 - x)$$

This leaves **one unknown** ($a_2$), so we need **one collocation point**. However, since the problem states two points, we will use a 3-term trial function for a more general approach. Let us redo with two unknowns.

### Step 1 (Revised) — Three-Term Trial Function

Let us use the polynomial basis that satisfies the BCs:

$$\tilde{y}(x) = x + a_1 \, x(1-x) + a_2 \, [x(1-x)]^2$$

So we have **two unknowns** $a_1, a_2$ and we will use **two collocation points** $x_1 = 1/3$, $x_2 = 2/3$.

### Step 2 — Compute the Derivatives

Let $g(x) = x(1-x) = x - x^2$.

$$\frac{dg}{dx} = 1 - 2x, \quad \frac{d^2 g}{dx^2} = -2$$

Let $h(x) = [x(1-x)]^2 = (x - x^2)^2 = x^2 - 2x^3 + x^4$.

$$\frac{dh}{dx} = 2x - 6x^2 + 4x^3$$

$$\frac{d^2 h}{dx^2} = 2 - 12x + 12x^2$$

The second derivative of the trial function is:

$$\frac{d^2 \tilde{y}}{dx^2} = 0 + a_1(-2) + a_2(2 - 12x + 12x^2)$$

$$\frac{d^2 \tilde{y}}{dx^2} = -2a_1 + a_2(2 - 12x + 12x^2)$$

### Step 3 — Form the Residual

$$R(x) = \frac{d^2 \tilde{y}}{dx^2} - 4\tilde{y}$$

$$R(x) = -2a_1 + a_2(2 - 12x + 12x^2) - 4[x + a_1 x(1-x) + a_2 x^2(1-x)^2]$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate Residual at $x_1 = 1/3$

At $x = 1/3$:

$$x(1-x) = \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \tfrac{2}{3} = \tfrac{2}{9}$$

$$x^2(1-x)^2 = \tfrac{4}{81}$$

$$12x^2 = 12 \cdot \tfrac{1}{9} = \tfrac{4}{3}$$

Substituting:

$$R(1/3) = -2a_1 + a_2\left(2 - 4 + \tfrac{4}{3}\right) - 4\left[\tfrac{1}{3} + a_1 \cdot \tfrac{2}{9} + a_2 \cdot \tfrac{4}{81}\right]$$

$$R(1/3) = -2a_1 + a_2\left(-2 + \tfrac{4}{3}\right) - \tfrac{4}{3} - \tfrac{8a_1}{9} - \tfrac{16 a_2}{81}$$

$$R(1/3) = a_1\left(-2 - \tfrac{8}{9}\right) + a_2\left(-\tfrac{2}{3} - \tfrac{16}{81}\right) - \tfrac{4}{3}$$

$$R(1/3) = -\tfrac{26}{9} a_1 - \tfrac{70}{81} a_2 - \tfrac{4}{3} = 0$$

Multiplying through by 81:

$$-234 \, a_1 - 70 \, a_2 - 108 = 0$$

$$\boxed{234 \, a_1 + 70 \, a_2 = -108} \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate Residual at $x_2 = 2/3$

At $x = 2/3$:

$$x(1-x) = \tfrac{2}{3} \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} = \tfrac{2}{9}$$

$$x^2(1-x)^2 = \tfrac{4}{81}$$

$$12x^2 = 12 \cdot \tfrac{4}{9} = \tfrac{16}{3}$$

Substituting:

$$R(2/3) = -2a_1 + a_2\left(2 - 8 + \tfrac{16}{3}\right) - 4\left[\tfrac{2}{3} + a_1 \cdot \tfrac{2}{9} + a_2 \cdot \tfrac{4}{81}\right]$$

$$R(2/3) = -2a_1 + a_2\left(-6 + \tfrac{16}{3}\right) - \tfrac{8}{3} - \tfrac{8a_1}{9} - \tfrac{16 a_2}{81}$$

$$R(2/3) = a_1\left(-2 - \tfrac{8}{9}\right) + a_2\left(-\tfrac{2}{3} - \tfrac{16}{81}\right) - \tfrac{8}{3}$$

$$R(2/3) = -\tfrac{26}{9} a_1 - \tfrac{70}{81} a_2 - \tfrac{8}{3} = 0$$

Multiplying through by 81:

$$-234 \, a_1 - 70 \, a_2 - 216 = 0$$

$$\boxed{234 \, a_1 + 70 \, a_2 = -216} \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$

### Step 6 — Solve the Linear System

Equations 1 and 2:

$$234 \, a_1 + 70 \, a_2 = -108$$
$$234 \, a_1 + 70 \, a_2 = -216$$

**This system is inconsistent!** The two equations have identical left-hand sides but different right-hand sides. This illustrates a critical lesson — see callout below.

> [!WARNING]
> **Important Observation:** The chosen trial function is symmetric about $x = 1/2$. Since the operator and boundary conditions are also symmetric in a sense, the two collocation points (which are symmetric: $1/3$ and $2/3$) yield **identical equations**. This is a numerical degeneracy. To fix this:
> 1. Use **asymmetric** collocation points (e.g., $x_1 = 0.25$, $x_2 = 0.75$).
> 2. Add an **asymmetric** term to the trial function (e.g., $x(1-x)(2x-1)$).
> 3. Increase the number of unknowns and collocation points.

### Step 1 (Final Corrected Attempt) — Use Asymmetric Trial Function

Let us use:

$$\tilde{y}(x) = x + a_1 \, x(1-x) + a_2 \, x^2(1-x)$$

Note: $x^2(1-x)$ is **asymmetric** about $x=1/2$.

Check BCs: $\tilde{y}(0) = 0$ ✓, $\tilde{y}(1) = 1 + 0 + 0 = 1$ ✓.

Derivatives:

$$\frac{d^2}{dx^2}[x^2(1-x)] = \frac{d^2}{dx^2}[x^2 - x^3] = 2 - 6x$$

$$\frac{d^2 \tilde{y}}{dx^2} = -2a_1 + a_2(2 - 6x)$$

$$4\tilde{y} = 4x + 4a_1 x(1-x) + 4a_2 x^2(1-x)$$

Residual:

$$R(x) = -2a_1 + a_2(2-6x) - 4x - 4a_1 x(1-x) - 4a_2 x^2(1-x)$$

**At $x_1 = 1/3$:**

$$x(1-x) = \tfrac{2}{9}, \quad x^2(1-x) = \tfrac{2}{27}$$

$$R(1/3) = -2a_1 + a_2(2-2) - \tfrac{4}{3} - 4a_1 \cdot \tfrac{2}{9} - 4a_2 \cdot \tfrac{2}{27}$$

$$R(1/3) = -2a_1 + 0 - \tfrac{4}{3} - \tfrac{8a_1}{9} - \tfrac{8a_2}{27} = 0$$

$$-\tfrac{26}{9} a_1 - \tfrac{8}{27} a_2 = \tfrac{4}{3}$$

Multiplying by 27:

$$-78 \, a_1 - 8 \, a_2 = 36 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 78 a_1 + 8 a_2 = -36 \quad \text{...(I)}$$

**At $x_2 = 2/3$:**

$$x(1-x) = \tfrac{2}{9}, \quad x^2(1-x) = \tfrac{4}{27}$$

$$R(2/3) = -2a_1 + a_2(2-4) - \tfrac{8}{3} - 4a_1 \cdot \tfrac{2}{9} - 4a_2 \cdot \tfrac{4}{27}$$

$$R(2/3) = -2a_1 - 2a_2 - \tfrac{8}{3} - \tfrac{8a_1}{9} - \tfrac{16 a_2}{27} = 0$$

$$-\tfrac{26}{9} a_1 - a_2\left(2 + \tfrac{16}{27}\right) = \tfrac{8}{3}$$

$$-\tfrac{26}{9} a_1 - \tfrac{70}{27} a_2 = \tfrac{8}{3}$$

Multiplying by 27:

$$-78 \, a_1 - 70 \, a_2 = 72 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 78 a_1 + 70 a_2 = -72 \quad \text{...(II)}$$

### Step 7 — Solve the Corrected System

$$78 a_1 + 8 a_2 = -36 \quad \text{...(I)}$$
$$78 a_1 + 70 a_2 = -72 \quad \text{...(II)}$$

Subtracting (I) from (II):

$$62 \, a_2 = -36 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_2 = -\tfrac{36}{62} = -\tfrac{18}{31}$$

Substituting back into (I):

$$78 a_1 + 8\left(-\tfrac{18}{31}\right) = -36$$

$$78 a_1 - \tfrac{144}{31} = -36$$

$$78 a_1 = -36 + \tfrac{144}{31} = \tfrac{-1116 + 144}{31} = -\tfrac{972}{31}$$

$$a_1 = -\tfrac{972}{31 \cdot 78} = -\tfrac{972}{2418} = -\tfrac{18}{44.78} \approx -\tfrac{162}{403}$$

### Step 8 — Final Approximate Solution

$$\tilde{y}(x) = x - \tfrac{18}{31} x(1-x) - \tfrac{162}{2418} x^2(1-x)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Compare with the **exact solution**. The exact solution to $y'' - 4y = 0$ with $y(0)=0$, $y(1)=1$ is $y(x) = \dfrac{\sinh(2x)}{\sinh(2)} \approx \dfrac{e^{2x} - e^{-2x}}{e^2 - e^{-2}}$. At $x=0.5$, $y_{exact} \approx 0.4236$. The collocation approximation should be reasonably close, demonstrating that even with 2 points, the method gives acceptable engineering accuracy.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Why Collocation Can Fail

| Aspect | Collocation (with bad points) | Collocation (with good points) | Galerkin |
| :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| Stability | Can be unstable | Moderate | Stable |
| Symmetry preservation | Often lost | Depends on trial fn | Natural |
| Accuracy | Poor for high order | Good | Best |
| Computational cost | Lowest | Low | Medium |
| Convergence | Not guaranteed | Conditional | Guaranteed |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Collocation Method Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given BVP L y equals f x with BCs] --> B[Choose Trial Function y tilde with n unknown coefficients]
    B --> C{Trial Function Satisfies All Essential BCs?}
    C -- No --> D[Modify Trial Function by adding BC-compatible terms]
    D --> B
    C -- Yes --> E[Substitute y tilde into Governing Equation]
    E --> F[Compute Residual R of x]
    F --> G[Select n distinct Interior Collocation Points x1 to xn]
    G --> H[Apply Collocation Condition: R of xi equals 0 for all i]
    H --> I[Form System of n Linear Algebraic Equations in n Unknowns]
    I --> J{System Consistent and Solvable?}
    J -- No --> K[Re-examine choice of Trial Function and Collocation Points]
    K --> B
    J -- Yes --> L[Solve Linear System for coefficients c1 to cn]
    L --> M[Construct Approximate Solution y tilde of x]
    M --> N[Compare with exact or other WRM solutions]
    N --> O[End: Report Final Approximate Solution]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Differential Operator L] --> R[Residual Computation Module R of x]
    P2[Trial Function y tilde x] --> R
    P3[Source Term f of x] --> R
    R --> Q1{R of x at Collocation Point xi}
    Q1 -- Point 1 --> S1[Equation 1]
    Q1 -- Point 2 --> S2[Equation 2]
    Q1 -- Point n --> SN[Equation n]
    S1 --> SYS[Linear Algebraic System K c equals F]
    S2 --> SYS
    SN --> SYS
    SYS --> SOL[Solver: Gaussian Elimination or Matrix Inversion]
    SOL --> OUT[Coefficients c1 to cn]
    OUT --> FIN[Final Approximate Solution y approx]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Weighted Residual Family

```mermaid
graph TD
    WRM[Weighted Residual Methods WRM] --> COL[Collocation Method]
    WRM --> SUB[Subdomain Method]
    WRM --> LSQ[Least Squares Method]
    WRM --> GAL[Galerkin Method]
    WRM --> MOM[Moment Method]
    COL --> W1[Weight: Dirac Delta delta of x minus xi]
    SUB --> W2[Weight: Indicator Function]
    LSQ --> W3[Weight: partial R over partial ci]
    GAL --> W4[Weight: Trial Function N_i]
    MOM --> W5[Weight: Powers of x: 1, x, x squared]
    W1 --> PROP1[Residual Zero at Selected Points]
    W2 --> PROP2[Average Residual Zero over Subdomains]
    W3 --> PROP3[Minimizes Integral of R squared]
    W4 --> PROP4[Residual Orthogonal to Trial Space]
    W5 --> PROP5[Integral Moments of Residual Vanish]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
> *What is meant by the Collocation Method? State its weighting function.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The Collocation Method is a **Weighted Residual Method** for obtaining approximate solutions to differential equations. The approximate (trial) solution is chosen to satisfy the governing differential equation **exactly at a finite number of pre-selected interior points**, called **collocation points**. The method forces the residual to vanish at these points only.
>
> The weighting function in the Collocation Method is the **Dirac Delta function**:
> $$W_i(x) = \delta(x - x_i)$$
>
> This reduces the weighted residual integral to a pointwise evaluation:
> $$R(x_i) = 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$
>
> The number of collocation points equals the number of unknown coefficients in the trial function, yielding a solvable linear system.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
> *List any three advantages and two disadvantages of the Collocation Method over the Galerkin Method.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantages of Collocation over Galerkin:**
> 1. **Computational simplicity** — No integration is required; only pointwise residual evaluations.
> 2. **Easy implementation** — Generates a simple linear algebraic system of size $n \times n$.
> 3. **Suitable for irregular domains** — Points can be placed anywhere in the domain.
>
> **Disadvantages:**
> 1. **Lower global accuracy** — Satisfies the equation only at discrete points, not in an average or integrated sense.
> 2. **Sensitivity to point selection** — Poor choice of collocation points leads to inaccurate or even inconsistent systems.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Pattern]*
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] *Define the Weighted Residual Method. Explain the Collocation Method with its weighting function. State the step-by-step algorithm.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] *Using the Collocation Method, obtain an approximate solution to the following boundary value problem with two interior collocation points at $x = 0.25$ and $x = 0.75$:*
> $$\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + y = -x, \quad 0 < x < 1, \quad y(0) = 0, \quad y(1) = 0$$

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Trial Function:**
Since $y(0) = 0$ and $y(1) = 0$, a suitable basis is $\{x(1-x), x^2(1-x), \dots\}$. With two unknowns:

$$\tilde{y}(x) = a_1 \, x(1-x) + a_2 \, x^2(1-x)$$

**Step 2 — Second Derivative:**

$$\frac{d^2}{dx^2}[x(1-x)] = -2$$

$$\frac{d^2}{dx^2}[x^2(1-x)] = \frac{d^2}{dx^2}(x^2 - x^3) = 2 - 6x$$

$$\frac{d^2 \tilde{y}}{dx^2} = -2a_1 + a_2(2 - 6x)$$

**Step 3 — Residual:**

$$R(x) = \frac{d^2 \tilde{y}}{dx^2} + \tilde{y} + x$$

$$R(x) = -2a_1 + a_2(2-6x) + a_1 x(1-x) + a_2 x^2(1-x) + x$$

**Step 4 — Apply at $x_1 = 0.25$:**

$$x(1-x) = 0.1875, \quad x^2(1-x) = 0.046875$$

$$6x = 1.5$$

$$R(0.25) = -2a_1 + a_2(0.5) + 0.1875 a_1 + 0.046875 a_2 + 0.25$$

$$R(0.25) = -1.8125 \, a_1 + 0.546875 \, a_2 + 0.25 = 0$$

**Step 5 — Apply at $x_2 = 0.75$:**

$$x(1-x) = 0.1875, \quad x^2(1-x) = 0.140625$$

$$6x = 4.5$$

$$R(0.75) = -2a_1 + a_2(-2.5) + 0.1875 a_1 + 0.140625 a_2 + 0.75$$

$$R(0.75) = -1.8125 \, a_1 - 2.359375 \, a_2 + 0.75 = 0$$

**Step 6 — Solve:**

$$1.8125 \, a_1 - 0.546875 \, a_2 = 0.25 \quad \text{...(I)}$$

$$1.8125 \, a_1 + 2.359375 \, a_2 = 0.75 \quad \text{...(II)}$$

Subtracting (I) from (II):

$$2.90625 \, a_2 = 0.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_2 = \tfrac{0.5}{2.90625} \approx 0.1720$$

From (I):

$$1.8125 \, a_1 = 0.25 + 0.546875 \times 0.1720 \approx 0.25 + 0.0941 = 0.3441$$

$$a_1 \approx 0.1899$$

**Step 7 — Final Approximate Solution:**

$$\boxed{\tilde{y}(x) \approx 0.1899 \, x(1-x) + 0.1720 \, x^2(1-x)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison with exact solution:** The exact solution of $y'' + y = -x$ with $y(0) = y(1) = 0$ involves a particular integral plus homogeneous terms. At $x = 0.5$, exact $y \approx 0.0695$ (approximately), and the collocation value at $x=0.5$ gives $\tilde{y}(0.5) \approx 0.1899 \times 0.25 + 0.1720 \times 0.125 \approx 0.0475 + 0.0215 = 0.0690$. **Excellent agreement!**

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Pattern — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]*
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] *Compare the Collocation Method and the Galerkin Method with respect to weighting functions, formulation, and accuracy.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] *Using the Collocation Method, solve the initial value problem:*
> $$\frac{dy}{dx} - 2y = 3e^{2x}, \quad y(0) = 0, \quad 0 < x < 1$$
> *Use trial function $\tilde{y} = a_1 x + a_2 x^2$ and a single collocation point at $x = 0.5$.*

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Trial Function and BC:**

$$\tilde{y}(x) = a_1 x + a_2 x^2, \quad \tilde{y}(0) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Derivative:**

$$\frac{d\tilde{y}}{dx} = a_1 + 2a_2 x$$

**Step 3 — Residual:**

$$R(x) = (a_1 + 2a_2 x) - 2(a_1 x + a_2 x^2) - 3e^{2x}$$

$$R(x) = a_1 + 2a_2 x - 2a_1 x - 2a_2 x^2 - 3e^{2x}$$

**Step 4 — Apply at $x = 0.5$:**

$$e^{2 \times 0.5} = e \approx 2.71828$$

$$R(0.5) = a_1 + a_2 - a_1 - 0.5 a_2 - 3e = 0$$

$$0.5 a_2 - 3e = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_2 = 6e \approx 16.3097$$

**Step 5 — Inconsistency!**

We have one equation and two unknowns. This is because one collocation point is **insufficient**. We need either:
- Two collocation points with two unknowns, or
- One collocation point with one unknown.

**Re-solve with trial function $\tilde{y} = a_1 x$ and one collocation point at $x = 0.5$:**

$$R(x) = a_1 - 2a_1 x - 3e^{2x}$$

$$R(0.5) = a_1 - a_1 - 3e = -3e = 0 \quad \text{(Impossible!)}$$

**Corrected approach — Use $\tilde{y} = a_1 x + a_2 x^2$ and TWO collocation points $x_1 = 0.25$, $x_2 = 0.75$:**

At $x_1 = 0.25$:

$$R(0.25) = a_1 + 0.5 a_2 - 0.5 a_1 - 0.125 a_2 - 3e^{0.5} = 0$$

$$0.5 a_1 + 0.375 a_2 = 3e^{0.5} \approx 3 \times 1.6487 = 4.9461$$

At $x_2 = 0.75$:

$$R(0.75) = a_1 + 1.5 a_2 - 1.5 a_1 - 1.125 a_2 - 3e^{1.5} = 0$$

$$-0.5 a_1 + 0.375 a_2 = 3e^{1.5} \approx 3 \times 4.4817 = 13.4451$$

Solving the $2 \times 2$ system:

$$0.5 a_1 + 0.375 a_2 = 4.9461 \quad \text{...(I)}$$

$$-0.5 a_1 + 0.375 a_2 = 13.4451 \quad \text{...(II)}$$

Adding (I) and (II):

$$0.75 a_2 = 18.3912 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_2 \approx 24.5216$$

Subtracting (II) from (I):

$$a_1 = -8.4990$$

**Final Approximate Solution:**

$$\boxed{\tilde{y}(x) \approx -8.4990 \, x + 24.5216 \, x^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The exact solution of the ODE is $y(x) = 3x e^{2x}$. At $x = 0.5$, $y_{exact} = 1.5 \times e \approx 4.077$. The collocation gives $\tilde{y}(0.5) \approx -4.2495 + 6.1304 = 1.8809$. The approximation is **reasonable but not perfect**, illustrating the inherent accuracy limits of low-order collocation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where Students Lose Marks in Collocation Method Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to enforce boundary conditions in the trial function** (Loss: 2 marks) — The trial function MUST satisfy the essential (Dirichlet) BCs by construction. Always check both BCs explicitly.
>
> 2. **Choosing collocation points ON the boundary** (Loss: 1 mark) — Points must be strictly inside the domain. The problem statement may sometimes ask you to verify this.
>
> 3. **Mismatched number of unknowns and equations** (Loss: 2 marks) — Always verify that the number of collocation points equals the number of unknown coefficients.
>
> 4. **Inconsistent system due to symmetric trial function + symmetric points** (Loss: 3 marks) — As shown in our first worked example, this leads to a degenerate system. Choose asymmetric functions or asymmetric points.
>
> 5. **Arithmetic errors in computing derivatives** (Loss: 2 marks) — Re-derive carefully. Polynomial differentiation is the most common source of mistakes.
>
> 6. **Not reporting the final answer in the form $\tilde{y}(x) = \dots$** (Loss: 1 mark) — Always state the final approximate solution explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collocation Method — High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**
>
> - **Definition:** WRM that forces residual to vanish at discrete interior points.
> - **Weighting function:** Dirac Delta $W_i = \delta(x - x_i)$.
> - **Collocation condition:** $R(x_i) = 0$ for $i = 1, \dots, n$.
> - **Number of conditions:** Always equal to number of unknown coefficients.
> - **Trial function** must satisfy all essential (Dirichlet) boundary conditions.
> - **Standard polynomial basis for $y(0) = y(1) = 0$:** $\{x(1-x), x^2(1-x), x^2(1-x)^2, \dots\}$.
> - **Standard polynomial basis for $y(0) = 0$, $y(1) = 1$:** $\{x, x(1-x), x^2(1-x), \dots\}$.
> - **Default collocation points:** Equally spaced interior points $x_i = i/(n+1)$.
> - **Advantages:** Simple, no integration, computationally cheap, easy to program.
> - **Disadvantages:** Lower global accuracy, sensitive to point choice, may yield inconsistent systems for symmetric problems.
> - **Avoid symmetric degeneracy:** When the trial function and domain are symmetric, use asymmetric collocation points.
> - **Resulting system:** A linear algebraic system $\mathbf{K} \mathbf{c} = \mathbf{F}$, solved by standard matrix methods.
> - **Comparison:** Collocation is the simplest WRM but the least globally accurate; Galerkin is the most widely used in FEM; Least Squares gives the best "best-fit" solution.
> - **Use cases:** Boundary element methods, mesh-free methods, spectral methods (with Chebyshev points).
> - **Step ordering to memorize:** Define ODE → Choose trial → Compute residual → Pick collocation points → Set $R(x_i) = 0$ → Solve system → Report $\tilde{y}(x)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Galerkin Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET757 - FINITE ELEMENT METHOD
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Galerkin Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Galerkin Method** is a systematic numerical technique, classified under the broader family of **Weighted Residual Methods (WRM)**, used to obtain approximate solutions to differential equations that cannot be solved analytically. Formally, it is a projection-based discretization approach in which the weighting (test) functions are chosen to be *identical* to the trial (shape) functions used to construct the approximate solution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *"Galerkin's method is a weighted residual technique in which the weighting functions are chosen to be the same as the basis/trial functions of the assumed approximate solution. This choice yields a symmetric, positive-definite algebraic system and forms the foundation of the standard Bubnov-Galerkin finite element formulation."*

The method was developed by the Russian mathematician **Boris Galerkin (1915)** and is the cornerstone of the finite element formulation taught in PECET757.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to estimate the height of a 50-storey building using only a measuring tape and a few reference sticks of known length.

- **The Building's True Height** → Exact solution $u(x)$ of the differential equation.
- **Your Reference Sticks** → Trial/approximation functions $N_i(x)$ multiplied by unknown coefficients $a_i$.
- **Your Best Guess** → The linear combination $\hat{u}(x) = \sum N_i(x) a_i$.
