Define a bar element. State the shape functions and derive its stiffness matrix. *(Conceptual — short version)* **Model Answer:** > A **bar element** is a 1-D, two-noded element with one DOF per node, used to model axial deformation. > > **Shape functions** (linear, in *x ∈ [0, L]*): > $$N_1 = 1 - \frac{x}{L}, \quad N_2 = \frac{x}{L}$$ > > **Strain-displacement matrix:** > $$[B] = \frac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ > > **Stiffness matrix:** > $$[k] = \int_0^L [B]^T E [B] A \, dx = \frac{EA}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ > > The matrix is **symmetric** and **singular** (det = 0) — singular because the bar can undergo **rigid body translation** without resistance. ### Q2. State the Hermite shape functions used for an Euler-Bernoulli beam element. *(Definition type)* **Model Answer:** > For a beam element with DOFs *{v₁, θ₁, v₂, θ₂}*, the Hermite shape functions (using *ξ = x/L*) are: > > $$N_1 = 1 - 3\xi^2 + 2\xi^3 \quad (\text{transverse at node 1})$$ > > $$N_2 = L(\xi - 2\xi^2 + \xi^3) \quad (\text{rotation at node 1})$$ > > $$N_3 = 3\xi^2 - 2\xi^3 \quad (\text{transverse at node 2})$$ > > $$N_4 = L(-\xi^2 + \xi^3) \quad (\text{rotation at node 2})$$ > > These functions are **C¹ continuous** (value and first derivative continuous), essential for beams since both deflection and slope must be continuous. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **A stepped bar ABCD is fixed at A and free at D, with cross-sectional areas A₁ = 600 mm² (AB = 200 mm), A₂ = 300 mm² (BC = 200 mm), and A₃ = 300 mm² (CD = 200 mm). E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm². An axial load P = 30 kN is applied at C. Determine:** **(a) The stiffness matrix of each bar element, the global stiffness matrix, and assemble it. (7 marks)** **(b) The nodal displacements and the reactions at the fixed support. (7 marks)** > *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 style]* `Model Question` #### Solution (a) — Stiffness & Global Assembly **Element Properties:** | Element | Length L (mm) | Area A (mm²) | AE/L (N/mm) | |---------|---------------|--------------|-------------| | AB | 200 | 600 | 6×10⁵ | | BC | 200 | 300 | 3×10⁵ | | CD | 200 | 300 | 3×10⁵ | **Element Stiffness Matrices:** $$[k]_{AB} = 6 \times 10^5 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad [k]_{BC} = 3 \times 10^5 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad [k]_{CD} = 3 \times 10^5 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Global Stiffness Assembly (4 nodes, 4 DOFs: u₁, u₂, u₃, u₄):** $$[K] = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -6 & 0 & 0 \\ -6 & 6+3 & -3 & 0 \\ 0 & -3 & 3+3 & -3 \\ 0 & 0 & -3 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}$$ $$[K] = 10^5 \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -6 & 0 & 0 \\ -6 & 9 & -3 & 0 \\ 0 & -3 & 6 & -3 \\ 0 & 0 & -3 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/mm}$$ #### Solution (b) — Displacements & Reactions **Boundary Conditions:** - *u₁ = 0* (fixed at A) - Applied load at C: *F₃ = -30,000 N* (compression if pushing inward, +ve if tensile) **Reduced System** (eliminating *u₁*): $$10^5 \begin{bmatrix} 9 & -3 & 0 \\ -3 & 6 & -3 \\ 0 & -3 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_2 \\ u_3 \\ u_4 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ -30000 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}$$ Dividing by 10⁵: $$\begin{bmatrix} 9 & -3 & 0 \\ -3 & 6 & -3 \\ 0 & -3 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_2 \\ u_3 \\ u_4 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ -0.3 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}$$ **Solving using Gaussian elimination:** - From R₁: 9u₂ − 3u₃ = 0 → u₃ = 3u₂ - From R₂: −3u₂ + 6(3u₂) − 3u₄ = −0.3 → 15u₂ − 3u₄ = −0.3 - From R₃: −3(3u₂) + 3u₄ = 0 → u₄ = 3u₂ Substituting into modified R₂: 15u₂ − 9u₂ = −0.3 → 6u₂ = −0.3 → **u₂ = −0.05 mm** Therefore: - **u₁ = 0** - **u₂ = −0.05 mm** (compression in AB) - **u₃ = 3u₂ = −0.15 mm** - **u₄ = 3u₂ = −0.15 mm** **Reaction at A** (from first equation of full system): $$F_1 = 6 \times 10^5 (0) - 6 \times 10^5(-0.05) = +30{,}000 \text{ N} = 30 \text{ kN}$$ The reaction is **30 kN** (tensile / restoring), balancing the applied load. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **A simply supported beam AB of length L = 4 m carries a uniformly distributed load w = 20 kN/m over its entire span. Use E = 2 × 10⁸ kN/m² and I = 4 × 10⁻⁴ m⁴. Using two beam elements of equal length:** **(a) Assemble the global stiffness matrix and force vector, and write the equilibrium equation. (7 marks)** **(b) Apply boundary conditions, solve for nodal rotations and deflections, and compute the bending moment at the mid-span. (7 marks)** > *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 style]* #### Solution (a) — Stiffness & Force Vector **Element data:** *L = 2 m*, *EI = 2×10⁸ × 4×10⁻⁴ = 8×10⁴ kN·m²* **Element Stiffness Matrix (single):** $$[k]^e = \frac{EI}{L^3}\begin{bmatrix} 12 & 6L & -12 & 6L \\ 6L & 4L^2 & -6L & 2L^2 \\ -12 & -6L & 12 & -6L \\ 6L & 2L^2 & -6L & 4L^2 \end{bmatrix}$$ Substituting *EI = 8×10⁴*, *L = 2*: $$[k]^e = \frac{8 \times 10^4}{8}\begin{bmatrix} 12 & 12 & -12 & 12 \\ 12 & 16 & -12 & 8 \\ -12 & -12 & 12 & -12 \\ 12 & 8 & -12 & 16 \end{bmatrix} = 10^4\begin{bmatrix} 12 & 12 & -12 & 12 \\ 12 & 16 & -12 & 8 \\ -12 & -12 & 12 & -12 \\ 12 & 8 & -12 & 16 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Equivalent Nodal Force Vector for UDL on each element** (length *L_e = 2 m*, load *w = 20 kN/m*): $$\{F\}_e = \begin{Bmatrix} wL_e/2 \\ wL_e^2/12 \\ wL_e/2 \\ -wL_e^2/12 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 20 \\ 6.667 \\ 20 \\ -6.667 \end{Bmatrix}$$ **Assembled Global System (6 DOFs: v₁, θ₁, v₂, θ₂, v₃, θ₃):** $$[K] = 10^4\begin{bmatrix} 12 & 12 & -12 & 12 & 0 & 0 \\ 12 & 16 & -12 & 8 & 0 & 0 \\ -12 & -12 & 24 & 0 & -12 & 12 \\ 12 & 8 & 0 & 32 & -12 & 8 \\ 0 & 0 & -12 & -12 & 12 & -12 \\ 0 & 0 & 12 & 8 & -12 & 16 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Global Force Vector:** $$\{F\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 20 \\ 6.667 \\ 40 \\ 0 \\ 20 \\ -6.667 \end{Bmatrix} \text{ kN, kN·m}$$ (The middle node *v₂, θ₂* gets contributions from both adjacent elements.) #### Solution (b) — Solve and Compute Moment **Boundary Conditions:** *v₁ = 0*, *v₃ = 0* (simply supported) **Reduced System** (active DOFs: *θ₁, v₂, θ₂*): $$10^4\begin{bmatrix} 16 & -12 & 8 \\ -12 & 24 & 0 \\ 8 & 0 & 32 \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} \theta_1 \\ v_2 \\ \theta_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 6.667 \\ 40 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}$$ Dividing by 10⁴: $$\begin{bmatrix} 16 & -12 & 8 \\ -12 & 24 & 0 \\ 8 & 0 & 32 \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} \theta_1 \\ v_2 \\ \theta_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \\ 4 \times 10^{-3} \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}$$ **Solution by matrix inversion (or Cramer's rule):** Solving the linear system: $$\theta_1 = -1.389 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}, \quad v_2 = 1.389 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}, \quad \theta_2 = 3.472 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad}$$ **Bending Moment at Mid-span** (from element 1, at *x = L_e/2 = 1 m from node 1*): Using *M = EI·v''(x)* with shape functions, at the midspan of element 1 (*ξ = 0.5*): $$M(1) = EI \cdot \frac{1}{L^2}\left[(6-12\xi)v_1 + L(4-6\xi)\theta_1 + (-6+12\xi)v_2 + L(2-6\xi)\theta_2\right]$$ At *ξ = 0.5*: coefficients become [0, L(4−3) = L, 0, L(2−3) = −L] $$M(1) = \frac{EI}{L^2}\left[L\theta_1 - L\theta_2\right] = \frac{EI}{L}(\theta_1 - \theta_2)$$ $$M(1) = \frac{8 \times 10^4}{2}(-1.389 \times 10^{-4} - 3.472 \times 10^{-5}) = 4 \times 10^4 \times (-1.736 \times 10^{-4})$$ $$M(1) = -6.944 \text{ kN·m}$$ > **Comparison with exact theory:** *M_max* (simply supported, UDL) = *wL²/8* = 20(4)²/8 = **40 kN·m** > > *Note: Our mid-span here is the midpoint of element 1 (at x=1m), not the beam midspan (x=2m). At beam midspan, |M| = 40 kN·m by symmetry of loading.* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers!** > 1. **Forgetting the consistent load vector**: Many students compute only point loads. UDL/wedge loads **MUST** be converted to equivalent nodal forces (e.g., *wL/2* and *wL²/12* for beams). Lose **2 marks** if missed. > 2. **Sign errors in [B] matrix for beam**: The double derivative sign for Hermite functions is tricky. Stick to the standard convention *M = EI·v''* to avoid sign flips. Lose **1–2 marks** per such error. > 3. **Global assembly errors**: The diagonal entry must equal the **sum of contributions from ALL elements meeting at that node**; off-diagonal terms at the interface must be **negative** of the corresponding *kᵢⱼ* of the contributing element. Use direct stiffness method carefully. > 4. **Forgetting units**: *EA/L* is in N/m or N/mm, *EI/L³* in N/m or N·m/rad. Mixing units (e.g., m and mm) is a **1-mark deduction** per KTU guidelines. > 5. **For beam elements, NOT including slope DOFs**: A 2-DOF bar cannot be used to model a beam — beam elements MUST have 4 DOFs. Lose **full marks** for using the wrong element. > 6. **Higher order elements — missing internal node DOFs**: A 3-node bar has **3** nodal DOFs (not 2). Always count nodes, not ends. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4** ### 🔹 Bar Element - ☐ 2 nodes, **2 DOFs total** (1 per node: *u₁, u₂*). - ☐ **Linear shape functions**: *N₁ = 1 − x/L*, *N₂ = x/L*. - ☐ Strain-displacement: **[B] = (1/L)[−1, 1]**. - ☐ Stiffness: **[k] = (EA/L) [[1, −1], [−1, 1]]**. - ☐ Properties: Symmetric, Singular (det = 0), Positive Semi-Definite. - ☐ Consistent nodal force (point load *P* at *x=a*): *F₁ = P(1 − a/L)*, *F₂ = P(a/L)*. ### 🔹 Beam Element - ☐ 2 nodes, **4 DOFs total** (2 per node: *v, θ*). - ☐ **Hermite shape functions** — cubic polynomial, *C¹* continuity. - ☐ Stiffness: **[k] = (EI/L³)** × standard 4×4 matrix with entries {12, 6L, 4L², 2L²}. - ☐ Equivalent nodal force for UDL: *F_v = wL/2*, *F_θ = wL²/12*. - ☐ Slope at free end of cantilever with tip load *P*: *θ = PL²/(2EI)*. ### 🔹 Higher Order Elements - ☐ **Lagrange family** (value-only DOFs): Linear (2 nodes), Quadratic (3), Cubic (4). - ☐ **Hermite family** (value + derivative DOFs): Cubic Hermite (2 nodes, 4 DOFs). - ☐ **Completeness**: Element must represent rigid body motion + constant strain exactly. - ☐ **Convergence**: As *h → 0*, FEM solution → exact solution. - ☐ **Pascal's triangle** dictates polynomial completeness in 1-D. ### 🔹 Universal Properties of Element Stiffness Matrices - ☐ **Symmetric**: *kᵢⱼ = kⱼᵢ* (Maxwell–Betti reciprocal theorem). - ☐ **Singular** (before applying BCs) — represents rigid body motion. - ☐ **Band-shaped** global K (bandwidth = max |i − j| + 1 over all elements). - ☐ **Positive semi-definite** (≥ 0 eigenvalues, all real). ### 🔹 Exam Triggers (KTU-Style Keywords → Method) - "**derive stiffness matrix**" → Use *K = ∫ Bᵀ D B dV*. - "**assemble global K**" → Direct stiffness method, sum overlapping entries. - "**equivalent nodal forces**" → Use consistent load vector: *F = ∫ Nᵀ b dV*. - "**symmetry and singularity**" → Always mention these two properties. - "**higher order elements / why?**" → Better accuracy, curved boundaries, gradient capture. > **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"S² P² R"** for stiffness properties — **S**ymmetric, **S**ingular, **P**ositive semi-definite, **P**roper units, **R**eal eigenvalues.

topics.title: Introduction to Axisymmetric Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET757 - FINITE ELEMENT METHOD
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Axisymmetric Elements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4 — Introduction to Axisymmetric Elements

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Axisymmetric Elements** are special two-dimensional finite elements used to analyze **three-dimensional bodies of revolution** in which the **geometry, material properties, loading, and boundary conditions** are all symmetric about a common axis of revolution.

When a 2D cross-section in the meridional $r$–$z$ plane is rotated 360° about the axis of symmetry, it sweeps out the entire 3D body. Because of this rotational symmetry, the field variables (displacement, strain, stress) are independent of the circumferential coordinate $\theta$, and the problem reduces from 3D to an effective **2D analysis** in the $r$–$z$ plane.

| Parameter | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| Coordinate system used | Cylindrical $(r, \theta, z)$ |
| Axis of symmetry | The $z$-axis (or $r = 0$ line) |
| Independent variables | $r$ and $z$ only |
| Dependent (eliminated) variable | Circumferential angle $\theta$ |
| Standard element type | 4-noded quadrilateral (Q4) or 3-noded triangle |
| Degrees of freedom per node | **2** (radial $u_r$ and axial $u_z$) |
| Total DOF for Q4 element | **8** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Plane Stress / Plane Strain:** Although axisymmetric problems are also solved in 2D, the hoop (circumferential) strain $\varepsilon_{\theta} = u_r / r$ is non-zero. This term has no counterpart in plane stress or plane strain formulations and is the **mathematical signature** of an axisymmetric problem.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a **cylindrical pressure vessel** such as a domestic LPG gas cylinder or a rocket motor casing. Its shape, the internal pressure pushing outward, and the way it is supported at the ends are all completely symmetric around its central vertical axis.

If you slice the cylinder vertically through the axis, you get a flat 2D rectangle. The full 3D stress–strain behavior of the cylinder is fully encoded in this 2D slice — provided you correctly account for the **curvature** of the geometry (which introduces the hoop strain term).

**Real-world engineering components that are axisymmetric:**

- **Pressure vessels, LPG cylinders, storage tanks**
- **Pipes, nozzles, flanges, reducers**
- **Pistons, valves, turbine disks**
- **Rocket motor casings and nozzles**
- **Flywheels, rotating disks, brake drums**
- **Dams (gravity dams approximated as axisymmetric)**
- **Forging and extrusion dies**

### 1.3 Why We Need Special Axisymmetric Elements

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A standard plane stress or plane strain element, when applied to a body of revolution, **gives wrong answers** because it cannot capture the **hoop stress $\sigma_{\theta}$** generated by circumferential stretching. The axisymmetric formulation inherently handles this.

The body is discretized in the $r$–$z$ plane using ordinary 2D elements, but each element represents a **3D ring of material** obtained by revolving the element about the $z$-axis. The volume of this ring is $dV = 2\pi r \, dr \, dz$ — this factor $2\pi r$ modifies the stiffness matrix formulation and is unique to axisymmetric FEM.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions for Axisymmetric Analysis

For the body to be treated as axisymmetric, **all four** of the following must hold:

1. The **geometry** is symmetric about an axis (body of revolution).
2. The **material properties** are symmetric (typically isotropic, homogeneous).
3. The **applied loads** are symmetric about the axis (e.g., internal/external pressure, body forces along $r$ or $z$).
4. The **boundary conditions (supports)** are symmetric about the axis.

If any of these is violated (for example, an asymmetric hole or an off-axis load), the problem is no longer axisymmetric and must be solved as a full 3D problem.

## 2.2 Displacement Field

Each node has **2 translational degrees of freedom**: radial displacement $u_r$ (or simply $u$) and axial displacement $u_z$ (or $w$). For a Q4 (4-noded quadrilateral) element:

$$
u(r,z) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i(r,z)\, u_i \qquad\qquad w(r,z) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i(r,z)\, w_i
$$

The shape functions $N_i$ are bilinear (the same as for a 2D plane element) expressed in the natural $(\xi, \eta)$ coordinate system.

## 2.3 Strain–Displacement Relationship

The four non-zero strain components in cylindrical coordinates for an axisymmetric body are:

$$
\varepsilon_r = \frac{\partial u}{\partial r}, \quad \varepsilon_z = \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}, \quad \varepsilon_{\theta} = \frac{u}{r}, \quad \gamma_{rz} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial r}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The circumferential displacement $v_{\theta}$ is **zero by symmetry**, and strains involving $\theta$-derivatives vanish.

In matrix form:

$$
\{\varepsilon\} = 
\begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_r \\ \varepsilon_z \\ \varepsilon_{\theta} \\ \gamma_{rz} \end{Bmatrix}
= \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix}
\frac{\partial N_1}{\partial r} & 0 & \frac{\partial N_2}{\partial r} & 0 & \cdots & \frac{\partial N_4}{\partial r} & 0 \\[4pt]
0 & \frac{\partial N_1}{\partial z} & 0 & \frac{\partial N_2}{\partial z} & \cdots & 0 & \frac{\partial N_4}{\partial z} \\[4pt]
\frac{N_1}{r} & 0 & \frac{N_2}{r} & 0 & \cdots & \frac{N_4}{r} & 0 \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial N_1}{\partial z} & \frac{\partial N_1}{\partial r} & \frac{\partial N_2}{\partial z} & \frac{\partial N_2}{\partial r} & \cdots & \frac{\partial N_4}{\partial z} & \frac{\partial N_4}{\partial r}
\end{bmatrix}}_{[B]}
\begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ w_1 \\ u_2 \\ w_2 \\ \vdots \\ u_4 \\ w_4 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

The matrix $[B]$ can be split into $[B] = [B_i \;\; B_j \;\; B_m \;\; B_p]$ where each $2 \times 2$ submatrix corresponds to a node.

## 2.4 Stress–Strain Relationship (Constitutive Matrix $[D]$)

For a **linear, isotropic, elastic** material, the constitutive matrix $[D]$ relates the four strain components to four stress components $\{\sigma\} = \{\sigma_r, \sigma_z, \sigma_{\theta}, \tau_{rz}\}^T$:

$$
[D] = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}
\begin{bmatrix}
1-\nu & \nu   & \nu   & 0 \\
\nu   & 1-\nu & \nu   & 0 \\
\nu   & \nu   & 1-\nu & 0 \\
0     & 0     & 0     & \dfrac{1-2\nu}{2}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

where $E$ is **Young's Modulus** and $\nu$ is **Poisson's Ratio**.

## 2.5 Element Stiffness Matrix

The element stiffness matrix for an axisymmetric element has the same algebraic form as a 2D plane element, but with an **extra factor of $2\pi r$** that accounts for revolution of the element about the symmetry axis:

$$
[k]^e = 2\pi \int_{-1}^{1}\!\int_{-1}^{1} [B]^T [D] [B]\, r \, \vert J \vert \, d\xi \, d\eta
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor $r$ inside the integrand (and $2\pi$ outside) is the **fingerprint of axisymmetric FEM**. This makes the element stiffness matrix **dependent on the geometric position $r$** — elements closer to the axis contribute less volume than elements far from the axis.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Displacements | $u = \sum N_i u_i,\;\; w = \sum N_i w_i$ | Q4 bilinear shape functions |
| 2 | Radial strain | $\varepsilon_r = \partial u / \partial r$ | In-plane derivative |
| 3 | Axial strain | $\varepsilon_z = \partial w / \partial z$ | In-plane derivative |
| 4 | **Hoop strain** | $\varepsilon_{\theta} = u / r$ | **Axisymmetric signature** |
| 5 | Shear strain | $\gamma_{rz} = \partial u / \partial z + \partial w / \partial r$ | Standard in-plane shear |
| 6 | B-matrix size | $4 \times 8$ for Q4 element | 4 strain components $\times$ 8 DOF |
| 7 | D-matrix size | $4 \times 4$ | Symmetric for isotropic material |
| 8 | Stiffness matrix | $k^e = 2\pi \int\!\int B^T D B\, r\, dr\, dz$ | Note the $2\pi r$ term |
| 9 | Gauss quadrature | $2 \times 2$ points for Q4 | Standard for Q4 |
| 10 | Body force vector | $f^e = 2\pi \int\!\int N^T \mathbf{b}\, r\, dr\, dz$ | $\mathbf{b} = (b_r, b_z)^T$ |
| 11 | Traction (pressure) | $f_{pres}^e = 2\pi \int N^T p\, r\, ds$ | Applied on curved surface |
| 12 | DOF per node | **2** | $(u_r, u_z)$ |
| 13 | Non-zero stress comps. | $\sigma_r, \sigma_z, \sigma_{\theta}, \tau_{rz}$ | All four matter |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Axisymmetric FEM |
|---|---|
| **Pressure Vessel Design** | LPG cylinders, boilers, nuclear containment |
| **Aerospace** | Rocket nozzle throat analysis, missile casings |
| **Rotating Machinery** | Turbine rotors, flywheels, brake discs |
| **Civil Engineering** | Gravity dam cross-section, underground silos |
| **Manufacturing** | Forging dies, extrusion billets, pipe fittings |
| **Nuclear Industry** | Fuel rod cladding, reactor pressure vessels |

The **computational saving is massive**: a 3D mesh of millions of tetrahedra is replaced by a 2D mesh of hundreds of quadrilaterals.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Strain Vector in Cylindrical Coordinates

**Step 1 — Start with the displacement field in cylindrical coordinates.**

In cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$, an axisymmetric body has only two non-zero displacement components: $u_r$ (radial) and $u_z$ (axial). The circumferential component $u_{\theta} = 0$ by symmetry.

**Step 2 — Write the small-strain tensor in cylindrical coordinates.**

For an axisymmetric problem (no $\theta$-dependence), the four non-vanishing strain components are:

$$
\varepsilon_{rr} = \frac{\partial u_r}{\partial r}, \quad \varepsilon_{zz} = \frac{\partial u_z}{\partial z}, \quad \varepsilon_{\theta\theta} = \frac{u_r}{r}, \quad \gamma_{rz} = \frac{\partial u_r}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial u_z}{\partial r}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the source of the hoop strain term.**

The term $\varepsilon_{\theta\theta} = u_r / r$ arises because a point at radius $r$ moves radially by $u_r$; the circumference of the original circle was $2\pi r$, and after deformation it becomes $2\pi(r + u_r)$. The change in circumference divided by the original gives:

$$
\varepsilon_{\theta\theta} = \frac{2\pi(r+u_r) - 2\pi r}{2\pi r} = \frac{u_r}{r}
$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the strain vector in the order $\{\varepsilon\} = \{\varepsilon_r, \varepsilon_z, \varepsilon_{\theta}, \gamma_{rz}\}^T$.**

$$
\{\varepsilon\} = \left\{ \frac{\partial u}{\partial r},\;\; \frac{\partial w}{\partial z},\;\; \frac{u}{r},\;\; \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial r} \right\}^{T}
$$

where we use the shorthand $u = u_r$ and $w = u_z$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the $[B]$ Matrix for a Q4 Axisymmetric Element

**Step 1 — Discretize the displacement using bilinear shape functions.**

For a 4-noded quadrilateral with nodes $i, j, m, p$ in counter-clockwise order:

$$
u(r,z) = N_i u_i + N_j u_j + N_m u_m + N_p u_p
$$
$$
w(r,z) = N_i w_i + N_j w_j + N_m w_m + N_p w_p
$$

**Step 2 — Express shape functions in natural coordinates $(\xi, \eta)$.**

$$
N_i = \tfrac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1-\eta), \quad N_j = \tfrac{1}{4}(1+\xi)(1-\eta)
$$
$$
N_m = \tfrac{1}{4}(1+\xi)(1+\eta), \quad N_p = \tfrac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1+\eta)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the derivatives of $N$ with respect to $r$ and $z$.**

Use the chain rule with the Jacobian $[J]$:

$$
\begin{Bmatrix} \frac{\partial N}{\partial r} \\[3pt] \frac{\partial N}{\partial z} \end{Bmatrix} = [J]^{-1} \begin{Bmatrix} \frac{\partial N}{\partial \xi} \\[3pt] \frac{\partial N}{\partial \eta} \end{Bmatrix}, \qquad [J] = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial r}{\partial \xi} & \frac{\partial z}{\partial \xi} \\[3pt] \frac{\partial r}{\partial \eta} & \frac{\partial z}{\partial \eta} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the $[B]$ matrix.**

The $[B]$ matrix is $4 \times 8$ (four strain components, eight DOF), partitioned into four $4 \times 2$ submatrices (one per node):

$$
[B] = \begin{bmatrix} B_i & B_j & B_m & B_p \end{bmatrix}
$$

For node $i$ (and analogously for $j, m, p$):

$$
B_i = \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial N_i}{\partial r} & 0 \\[8pt]
0 & \dfrac{\partial N_i}{\partial z} \\[8pt]
\dfrac{N_i}{r} & 0 \\[8pt]
\dfrac{\partial N_i}{\partial z} & \dfrac{\partial N_i}{\partial r}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Note the unique third row.**

Unlike plane elements, the third row contains $N_i / r$ (not a derivative). This forces the integrand to contain $N_i$ without a derivative, which is why the stiffness matrix has the factor $r$ multiplying the entire integrand.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Element Stiffness Matrix

**Step 1 — Start from the principle of minimum potential energy.**

The strain energy of a revolved element is:

$$
U^e = \frac{1}{2} \int_{V^e} \{\sigma\}^T \{\varepsilon\}\, dV
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\{\sigma\} = [D]\{\varepsilon\} = [D][B]\{d\}^e$ and $\{\varepsilon\} = [B]\{d\}^e$.**

$$
U^e = \frac{1}{2} \{d\}^{eT} \left( \int_{V^e} [B]^T [D] [B]\, dV \right) \{d\}^e
$$

**Step 3 — Convert the volume integral to the meridional plane.**

The volume element in cylindrical coordinates is $dV = r\, d\theta\, dr\, dz$. Integrating over the full revolution $\theta \in [0, 2\pi]$:

$$
\int_{V^e} (\cdot)\, dV = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\int_{A^e} (\cdot)\, r\, dr\, dz = 2\pi \int_{A^e} (\cdot)\, r\, dr\, dz
$$

**Step 4 — Final expression for the element stiffness matrix.**

$$
\boxed{\,[k]^e = 2\pi \int_{A^e} [B]^T [D] [B]\, r\, dr\, dz = 2\pi \int_{-1}^{1}\!\int_{-1}^{1} [B]^T [D] [B]\, r\, \vert J \vert \, d\xi\, d\eta\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral using Gauss quadrature.**

For a Q4 element, a $2 \times 2$ Gauss rule is sufficient:

$$
[k]^e \approx 2\pi \sum_{a=1}^{2}\sum_{b=1}^{2} W_a W_b \left([B(\xi_a, \eta_b)]^T [D] [B(\xi_a, \eta_b)]\right) r(\xi_a, \eta_b)\, \vert J(\xi_a, \eta_b) \vert
$$

where $W_a, W_b = 1$ are the Gauss weights and $(\xi_a, \eta_b) = (\pm 1/\sqrt{3}, \pm 1/\sqrt{3})$ are the Gauss points.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Illustration (Conceptual)

> Consider a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel of inner radius $r_i$, outer radius $r_o$, with internal pressure $p_i$. A 2D cross-section in the $r$–$z$ plane is a rectangle. Meshing with a single Q4 axisymmetric element, the $r$ values at Gauss points are between $r_i$ and $r_o$. The hoop stress recovered from the analysis is:

$$
\sigma_{\theta}(r) = \frac{p_i r_i^2 - p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} + \frac{r_i^2 r_o^2 (p_i - p_o)}{r\,(r_o^2 - r_i^2)}
$$

This is the **Lamé equation** for thick cylinders — recovered exactly by axisymmetric FEM, demonstrating the validity of the formulation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept of Revolution (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of how a 2D axisymmetric mesh represents a 3D body of revolution, including the key data transformations inside an FEM solver.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["3D BODY OF REVOLUTION"] --> nodeB["IDENTIFY AXIS OF SYMMETRY"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["EXTRACT 2D MERIDIONAL CROSS SECTION R-Z PLANE"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["DISCRETIZE WITH 2D ELEMENTS Q4 OR T3"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["ASSIGN 2 DOF PER NODE UR AND UZ"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["APPLY LOADS AND BOUNDARY CONDITIONS ON 2D MESH"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["COMPUTE B MATRIX INCLUDING HOOP STRAIN U BY R"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["FORM D MATRIX 4 BY 4 ISOTROPIC ELASTIC"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["INTEGRATE K EQUAL 2 PI INTEGRAL B T D B R DR DZ"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["ASSEMBLE GLOBAL K AND SOLVE U"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["RECOVER STRAINS EPSILON R Z THETA RZ"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["COMPUTE STRESSES SIGMA R Z THETA RZ"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["VISUALIZE RESULTS ON 2D MESH AND OPTIONALLY REVOLVE FOR 3D PLOT"]
```

## 4.2 Axisymmetric Mesh Discretization Example (Schematic)

The following diagram illustrates the **discretization hierarchy** — how a continuous axisymmetric body is represented by a 2D mesh, with each element implicitly representing a 3D ring of material.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["CONTINUOUS AXISYMMETRIC BODY"] --> nodeQ["2D CROSS SECTION IN R Z PLANE"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR["NODE NUMBERING WITH 2 DOF PER NODE"]
    nodeR --> nodeS["ELEMENT 1 Q4 QUAD"]
    nodeR --> nodeT["ELEMENT 2 Q4 QUAD"]
    nodeR --> nodeU["ELEMENT 3 Q4 QUAD"]
    nodeS --> nodeV["REVOLVED 3D RING SHELL ELEMENT"]
    nodeT --> nodeW["REVOLVED 3D RING SHELL ELEMENT"]
    nodeU --> nodeX["REVOLVED 3D RING SHELL ELEMENT"]
    nodeV --> nodeY["ASSEMBLED 3D MESH OF REVOLUTION"]
    nodeW --> nodeY
    nodeX --> nodeY
```

## 4.3 Comparison: Axisymmetric vs Plane Stress vs Plane Strain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeZ["2D FEM FORMULATIONS"] --> nodeAA["PLANE STRESS"]
    nodeZ --> nodeBB["PLANE STRAIN"]
    nodeZ --> nodeCC["AXISYMMETRIC"]
    nodeAA --> nodeDD["STRAINS EPS R EPS Z GAMMA RZ - 3 COMPONENTS"]
    nodeBB --> nodeDD
    nodeCC --> nodeEE["STRAINS EPS R EPS Z EPS THETA GAMMA RZ - 4 COMPONENTS"]
    nodeEE --> nodeFF["EXTRA HOOP STRAIN EPS THETA = U BY R"]
    nodeEE --> nodeGG["STIFFNESS FACTOR 2 PI R IN VOLUME INTEGRAL"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The third branch (axisymmetric) is fundamentally different because it carries a **fourth strain component** $\varepsilon_{\theta}$ and uses the **volumetric factor $2\pi r$** in the stiffness integration. The first two branches share the same $3 \times 3$ $[D]$ and the same $[B]$ structure (without the third row).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Short Answer — Conceptual)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q:** What is an axisymmetric element? State any two practical examples where it is applied.

**Model Answer:**

An axisymmetric element is a 2D finite element used to analyze a 3D body of revolution. The geometry, material, loading, and boundary conditions of the body are all symmetric about an axis (typically the $z$-axis). The 3D body is generated by revolving a 2D cross-section (in the $r$–$z$ plane) through 360° about this axis.

**Two practical examples:**

1. **Cylindrical pressure vessel** (e.g., LPG cylinder, boiler) — internal pressure loading.
2. **Rocket motor nozzle / casing** — internal pressure and thermal loads.

*(Valid alternatives: pipes, flywheels, pistons, turbine disks, forging dies, etc.)*

---

### Question 2 (Short Answer — Definition)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q:** Differentiate between an axisymmetric element and a plane strain element with respect to the strain components considered.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Plane Strain Element | Axisymmetric Element |
|---|---|---|
| Strain components | $\varepsilon_r, \varepsilon_z, \gamma_{rz}$ (3) | $\varepsilon_r, \varepsilon_z, \varepsilon_{\theta}, \gamma_{rz}$ (4) |
| Hoop strain $\varepsilon_{\theta}$ | **Absent** (zero) | **Present** $= u/r$ |
| Volume element in stiffness | $dr\, dz$ (unit depth) | $2\pi r\, dr\, dz$ (revolved) |
| Nature of 3D stress | Approximation (long body) | Exact (body of revolution) |
| Strain–displacement matrix | $3 \times 8$ | $4 \times 8$ (for Q4) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 4 Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
>
> - **Do NOT** use a 2D plane stress/strain $[B]$ matrix when solving axisymmetric problems — students commonly lose **3 to 4 marks** by omitting the **hoop strain row** $N_i / r$.
> - **Always** include the factor $2\pi r$ in the stiffness integration — losing this costs another **2 marks**.
> - **Always** show the size of the $[B]$ matrix ($4 \times 8$ for Q4) and the $[D]$ matrix ($4 \times 4$) explicitly in the answer.
> - **Do NOT** confuse $\sigma_{\theta}$ (hoop stress) with shear stress $\tau_{r\theta}$ — in axisymmetric problems $\tau_{r\theta} = 0$ by symmetry, but $\sigma_{\theta}$ is **not** zero.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, Module 4]**

**Q:** A long thick-walled cylindrical pressure vessel has inner radius $r_i = 50$ mm, outer radius $r_o = 100$ mm, and is subjected to internal pressure $p_i = 100$ MPa.

**(a)** Derive the strain–displacement $[B]$ matrix for a 4-noded axisymmetric quadrilateral element, clearly explaining the appearance of the hoop strain term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write down the constitutive matrix $[D]$ for an isotropic linear elastic material, and explain why the axisymmetric element stiffness matrix is integrated with a factor $2\pi r$. Show the final expression for $[k]^e$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Displacement field with bilinear shape functions.**

For a Q4 axisymmetric element with 4 nodes (each carrying 2 DOF: $u$ and $w$):

$$
u(r,z) = N_1 u_1 + N_2 u_2 + N_3 u_3 + N_4 u_4
$$
$$
w(r,z) = N_1 w_1 + N_2 w_2 + N_3 w_3 + N_4 w_4
$$

where $N_i(\xi, \eta) = \tfrac{1}{4}(1+\xi_i\xi)(1+\eta_i\eta)$.

**Step 2 — Strain components in cylindrical coordinates.**

$$
\varepsilon_r = \frac{\partial u}{\partial r}, \quad \varepsilon_z = \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}, \quad \varepsilon_{\theta} = \frac{u}{r}, \quad \gamma_{rz} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial r}
$$

**Step 3 — Origin of the hoop strain $\varepsilon_{\theta} = u/r$.**

A material point initially on a circle of radius $r$ moves radially by $u$. Its new circumference is $2\pi(r+u)$, while the original was $2\pi r$. The change in circumference per unit original length is:

$$
\varepsilon_{\theta} = \frac{2\pi(r+u) - 2\pi r}{2\pi r} = \frac{u}{r}
$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the $[B]$ matrix ($4 \times 8$).**

$$
[B] = \begin{bmatrix}
\frac{\partial N_1}{\partial r} & 0 & \frac{\partial N_2}{\partial r} & 0 & \frac{\partial N_3}{\partial r} & 0 & \frac{\partial N_4}{\partial r} & 0 \\
0 & \frac{\partial N_1}{\partial z} & 0 & \frac{\partial N_2}{\partial z} & 0 & \frac{\partial N_3}{\partial z} & 0 & \frac{\partial N_4}{\partial z} \\
\frac{N_1}{r} & 0 & \frac{N_2}{r} & 0 & \frac{N_3}{r} & 0 & \frac{N_4}{r} & 0 \\
\frac{\partial N_1}{\partial z} & \frac{\partial N_1}{\partial r} & \frac{\partial N_2}{\partial z} & \frac{\partial N_2}{\partial r} & \frac{\partial N_3}{\partial z} & \frac{\partial N_3}{\partial r} & \frac{\partial N_4}{\partial z} & \frac{\partial N_4}{\partial r}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Key Observation:** The third row contains $N_i/r$ — a term that has **no derivative**. This is the **mathematical signature** of the axisymmetric formulation, and it is **absent** in plane stress/strain elements. (7 marks satisfied)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Constitutive matrix $[D]$ for isotropic material.**

For a linear elastic isotropic material with Young's modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$:

$$
[D] = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}
\begin{bmatrix}
1-\nu & \nu & \nu & 0 \\
\nu & 1-\nu & \nu & 0 \\
\nu & \nu & 1-\nu & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & \frac{1-2\nu}{2}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The size of $[D]$ is $4 \times 4$ because there are four stress components $\sigma_r, \sigma_z, \sigma_{\theta}, \tau_{rz}$ and four corresponding strain components.

**Step 2 — Origin of the $2\pi r$ factor in stiffness integration.**

In cylindrical coordinates, the differential volume of an element revolved about the $z$-axis is:

$$
dV = r\, d\theta\, dr\, dz
$$

When the 2D element is revolved through the full angle $\theta \in [0, 2\pi]$, the volume integral becomes:

$$
\int_{V^e} (\cdot)\, dV = \int_0^{2\pi}\!\int_{A^e} (\cdot)\, r\, dr\, dz = 2\pi \int_{A^e} (\cdot)\, r\, dr\, dz
$$

**Step 3 — Final expression for the element stiffness matrix.**

$$
[k]^e = 2\pi \int_{A^e} [B]^T [D] [B]\, r\, dr\, dz
$$

Converting to natural coordinates $(\xi, \eta)$ via the Jacobian:

$$
\boxed{\,[k]^e = 2\pi \int_{-1}^{1}\!\int_{-1}^{1} [B(\xi,\eta)]^T [D] [B(\xi,\eta)]\, r(\xi,\eta)\, \vert J(\xi,\eta) \vert\, d\xi\, d\eta\,}
$$

The size of $[k]^e$ is $8 \times 8$ (2 DOF per node $\times$ 4 nodes). (7 marks satisfied)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Option 2** (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, Module 4]**

**Q:** A long pipe of inner radius $r_i = 80$ mm and outer radius $r_o = 120$ mm is subjected to an internal pressure $p_i = 80$ MPa and an external pressure $p_o = 20$ MPa. The pipe material has $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.3$. Use a **single 4-noded axisymmetric element** mesh.

**(a)** Write the displacement field, derive the $[B]$ and $[D]$ matrices (do not evaluate the integrals), and state the expression for the element stiffness matrix with proper units. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the special case of plane strain (i.e., set $\varepsilon_{\theta} = 0$), recover the $[B]$ matrix of a plane strain element from the axisymmetric $[B]$. Comment on the limiting case $r \to \infty$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Displacement field.**

$$
u(r,z) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i(r,z)\, u_i, \qquad w(r,z) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i(r,z)\, w_i
$$

**Step 2 — Strain–displacement $[B]$ matrix ($4 \times 8$).**

See derivation in Question A part (a). The matrix has 4 rows (strain components) and 8 columns (nodal DOF).

**Step 3 — Constitutive $[D]$ matrix ($4 \times 4$).**

$$
[D] = \frac{200{,}000}{(1.3)(0.4)}
\begin{bmatrix}
0.7 & 0.3 & 0.3 & 0 \\
0.3 & 0.7 & 0.3 & 0 \\
0.3 & 0.3 & 0.7 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0.2
\end{bmatrix}
\quad \text{(units: MPa)}
$$

Numerically:

$$
[D] \approx 384{,}615 \times
\begin{bmatrix}
0.7 & 0.3 & 0.3 & 0 \\
0.3 & 0.7 & 0.3 & 0 \\
0.3 & 0.3 & 0.7 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0.2
\end{bmatrix} \;\;\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Element stiffness matrix.**

$$
[k]^e = 2\pi \int_{-1}^{1}\!\int_{-1}^{1} [B]^T [D] [B]\, r\, \vert J \vert\, d\xi\, d\eta
$$

The final matrix is $8 \times 8$ with units of **N/mm** (or MPa·mm). (7 marks satisfied)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Set the hoop strain to zero.**

In axisymmetric $[B]$, the third row is $N_i / r$. For plane strain, we drop the $\varepsilon_{\theta}$ component entirely (because in plane strain $\varepsilon_{\theta} = 0$). The modified $[B]_{PS}$ becomes $3 \times 8$:

$$
[B]_{PS} = \begin{bmatrix}
\frac{\partial N_1}{\partial r} & 0 & \frac{\partial N_2}{\partial r} & 0 & \cdots & \frac{\partial N_4}{\partial r} & 0 \\
0 & \frac{\partial N_1}{\partial z} & 0 & \frac{\partial N_2}{\partial z} & \cdots & 0 & \frac{\partial N_4}{\partial z} \\
\frac{\partial N_1}{\partial z} & \frac{\partial N_1}{\partial r} & \frac{\partial N_2}{\partial z} & \frac{\partial N_2}{\partial r} & \cdots & \frac{\partial N_4}{\partial z} & \frac{\partial N_4}{\partial r}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Limiting case $r \to \infty$.**

As $r \to \infty$:

- The hoop strain $\varepsilon_{\theta} = u/r \to 0$ because the radius is enormous relative to the small radial displacement.
- The volume factor $2\pi r$ becomes enormous, but the body is locally approximated as flat.
- The element behaves like a **plane strain** element (small region of a very large radius body appears flat to local deformation).

**Conclusion:** Plane strain is the **limiting case of axisymmetric** as $r \to \infty$. Physically, a small patch of a huge sphere or cylinder looks like a flat 2D plane, and circumferential strain becomes negligible. (7 marks satisfied)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Points for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4: Axisymmetric Elements**

- **Axisymmetric element = 2D element representing a 3D body of revolution** (geometry, load, material, BC all symmetric about an axis).
- Each node has **2 DOF**: $u$ (radial) and $w$ (axial). Q4 element has **8 DOF total**.
- The four non-zero strain components are $\varepsilon_r, \varepsilon_z, \varepsilon_{\theta}, \gamma_{rz}$.
- **Hoop strain $\varepsilon_{\theta} = u/r$ is the unique signature** of axisymmetric formulation.
- $[B]$ matrix is $4 \times 8$ (for Q4); the third row contains $N_i / r$ (no derivative).
- $[D]$ matrix is $4 \times 4$ — symmetric for isotropic elastic material.
- **Stiffness matrix always includes the factor $2\pi r$** in the volume integral.
- Gauss quadrature: $2 \times 2$ rule is sufficient for Q4 axisymmetric element.
- **Common applications:** pressure vessels, pipes, rocket nozzles, flywheels, pistons, turbine disks, forging dies.
- **Limiting case $r \to \infty$:** axisymmetric → plane strain (hoop strain becomes negligible).
- **Common student mistake:** omitting the $2\pi r$ factor, or using a $3 \times 8$ plane $[B]$ instead of $4 \times 8$ axisymmetric $[B]$.
- **Examiner hot-spot:** deriving the $[B]$ matrix, writing $[D]$, and showing the final $[k]^e$ integral expression.
- **Total marks distribution for this topic in KTU 2024 ESE:** typically a full 14-mark Part B question, occasionally supplemented by a 3-mark Part A short question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Integration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET757 - FINITE ELEMENT METHOD
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Numerical Integration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Integration** is the computational procedure of approximating the definite integral of a function by evaluating the function at a finite set of strategically chosen sample points (nodes or stations) inside the domain, multiplying each evaluated value by a corresponding weighting coefficient, and summing the products.

In the context of the **Finite Element Method (FEM)**, numerical integration is *not* an optional convenience — it is the **operational engine** that converts continuous integrals (such as the element stiffness matrix entries) into discrete, computable algebraic sums. The general one-dimensional form is expressed as:

$$\int_{-1}^{+1} f(\xi) \, d\xi \;\approx\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_{i} \cdot f(\xi_{i})$$

where:
- $\xi_{i}$ = sample point (abscissa) of the $i^{\text{th}}$ station
- $W_{i}$ = weight factor associated with the $i^{\text{th}}$ station
- $n$ = total number of sampling points (order of the rule)
- $f(\xi)$ = the integrand (typically a polynomial constructed from shape functions)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why FEM Uses Numerical Integration:** Element matrices like $[K]^{e} = \int_{V^{e}} [B]^{T} [D] [B] \, dV$ involve polynomial integrands whose analytical integration, while possible, becomes extraordinarily tedious for higher-order elements, 3D solids, and curved isoparametric geometries. Numerical integration makes the problem tractable *without* sacrificing accuracy.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you want to find the **area under a curved speed–time graph** to compute the distance travelled by a car. You do not have the exact equation of the curve, but a tracking device records the speed at regular time intervals.

- **Analytical integration** = having a perfect mathematical formula for the curve and integrating it symbolically.
- **Trapezoidal rule** = connecting those recorded speed points with straight lines and computing the trapezoidal area — fast but approximate.
- **Simpson's rule** = fitting parabolic arcs through the recorded points — more accurate for the same number of readings.
- **Gauss quadrature** = a *clever twist*: instead of fixing the measurement stations at equal intervals, you strategically *move* the stations to "secret" positions and assign a *weight* to each, so that even with very few points you capture the area with near-perfect precision.

**Engineering Analogy:** It is like weighing a complex, irregularly shaped object on a digital balance. The balance samples the load at microsecond intervals, multiplies by the elapsed time, and integrates — yielding the impulse, just as Gauss quadrature samples $f(x)$ and weights it to yield the integral.

## 1.3 Key Classifications (Hierarchy of Methods)

| **Method Family** | **Sampling Strategy** | **Typical Use in FEM** |
|---|---|---|
| Newton–Cotes (Trapezoidal, Simpson's) | Equally-spaced fixed points | Rare in modern FEM; mostly for 1D hand-calculations |
| Gauss–Legendre Quadrature | Optimally placed, unequal spacing | **Standard for element matrix evaluation** |
| Gauss–Lobatto | Includes end-points $\pm 1$ | Boundary-condition enforcement, p-version FEM |
| Hammering (Triangular) | Special for triangles | 2D triangular CST/LST elements |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard FEM Convention:** Element matrices are *always* mapped to a parent (natural) domain $[-1, +1]$ in 1D, $[-1, +1] \times [-1, +1]$ in 2D, etc., because Gauss quadrature is formulated directly on these standardized limits.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Newton–Cotes Closed Quadrature (Foundational Rules)

These rules use **equally spaced points that include both endpoints** of the integration interval.

### 2.1.1 Trapezoidal Rule ($n = 2$)
The simplest of all — a single linear segment approximation:

$$I = \int_{a}^{b} f(x)\, dx \;\approx\; \frac{h}{2}\bigl[\,f(x_0) + f(x_1)\,\bigr], \quad h = b - a$$

- **Geometric meaning:** area of a single trapezium.
- **Accuracy:** Exact for polynomials up to degree **1**.
- **Error term:** $E = -\dfrac{(b-a)^{3}}{12}\,f''(\xi)$.

### 2.1.2 Simpson's 1/3 Rule ($n = 3$)
A parabolic arc fits through **three** equally spaced points:

$$I = \int_{a}^{b} f(x)\, dx \;\approx\; \frac{h}{3}\bigl[\,f(x_0) + 4 f(x_1) + f(x_2)\,\bigr], \quad h = \frac{b-a}{2}$$

- **Accuracy:** Exact for polynomials up to degree **3**.
- **Error term:** $E = -\dfrac{(b-a)^{5}}{180}\,f^{(4)}(\xi)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simpson's rule requires the number of sub-intervals to be even.** Always verify this in KTU numerical problems.

### 2.1.3 Simpson's 3/8 Rule ($n = 4$)
A cubic fit through **four** equally spaced points:

$$I \;\approx\; \frac{3h}{8}\bigl[\,f(x_0) + 3 f(x_1) + 3 f(x_2) + f(x_3)\,\bigr], \quad h = \frac{b-a}{3}$$

- **Accuracy:** Exact for polynomials up to degree **3**.
- **Often avoided** in FEM (Gauss quadrature is superior for the same number of evaluations).

## 2.2 Gauss–Legendre Quadrature (The FEM Workhorse)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Philosophy of Gauss Quadrature:** Both the locations ($\xi_i$) **and** the weights ($W_i$) are chosen *optimally* so that the rule integrates a polynomial of the highest possible degree **exactly**, given a fixed $n$.

The general form over the canonical domain $[-1, +1]$ is:

$$\int_{-1}^{+1} f(\xi)\, d\xi \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_{i}\, f(\xi_{i}) \;+\; E$$

A Gauss rule with $n$ sampling points integrates a polynomial of degree $\leq 2n-1$ **exactly**.

### 2.2.1 Derivation of 2-Point Gauss Rule (Pedagogical)
For $n = 2$, we have **four** unknowns: $\xi_1, \xi_2, W_1, W_2$. We impose that the rule integrate $1, \xi, \xi^{2}, \xi^{3}$ exactly (four conditions). By symmetry of the domain, $\xi_1 = -\xi_2$ and $W_1 = W_2$. Solving the system yields the canonical values.

## 2.3 Standard Gauss–Legendre Sampling Tables (MUST MEMORIZE)

### 2.3.1 One-Dimensional Gauss Points & Weights

| **Order $n$** | **Sampling Points $\xi_i$** | **Weights $W_i$** | **Polynomials Integrated Exactly** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $\xi_1 = 0$ | $W_1 = 2$ | up to degree 1 |
| 2 | $\xi_1 = -1/\sqrt{3}, \; \xi_2 = +1/\sqrt{3}$ | $W_1 = W_2 = 1$ | up to degree 3 |
| 3 | $\xi_1 = -\sqrt{3/5}, \; \xi_2 = 0, \; \xi_3 = +\sqrt{3/5}$ | $W_1 = W_3 = 5/9, \; W_2 = 8/9$ | up to degree 5 |
| 4 | $\pm 0.8611363, \; \pm 0.3399810$ | $0.3478551, \; 0.6521452$ (symmetric pairing) | up to degree 7 |
| 5 | $0, \pm 0.5384693, \pm 0.9061798$ | $0.5688889, \; 0.4786287, \; 0.2369269$ | up to degree 9 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorization Priority for KTU Exams:** 1-point, 2-point, and 3-point Gauss rules in 1D; 2$\times$2 and 3$\times$3 product rules in 2D. These are sufficient for the majority of board questions.

### 2.3.2 Two-Dimensional Gauss Quadrature (Square Parent Domain)
For a rectangular/quadrilateral element mapped to $[-1,+1] \times [-1,+1]$, the integral becomes a **tensor product** of two 1D rules:

$$\int_{-1}^{+1}\!\!\int_{-1}^{+1} f(\xi, \eta)\, d\xi\, d\eta \;\approx\; \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{m} W_{i}\, W_{j}\, f(\xi_i, \eta_j)$$

**Standard 2D Rules:**

| **Rule** | **Sampling Points (per direction)** | **Total Stations** | **Total DOF Evaluated** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $2 \times 2$ | $\pm 0.5774$ in each direction | **4** | 4 |
| $3 \times 3$ | $0, \pm 0.7746$ in each direction | **9** | 9 |
| $4 \times 4$ | $\pm 0.3399, \pm 0.8611$ in each direction | **16** | 16 |

## 2.4 Selection of Gauss Order in FEM — A Critical Decision

The number of Gauss points must be chosen such that the integrand is integrated **exactly**. For element matrices where the integrand is a polynomial of total degree $2p$ (where $p$ is the polynomial order of the shape functions), the minimum Gauss order required is $n = p + 1$ per direction (for 1D), or equivalently, $n$ such that $2n - 1 \geq 2p$.

| **Element Type** | **Shape Function Order $p$** | **Integrand Degree $2p$** | **Min. Gauss Order $n$** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1D Linear (2-node bar) | 1 | 2 | $n = 2$ |
| 1D Quadratic (3-node bar) | 2 | 4 | $n = 3$ |
| 2D Bilinear Q4 | 1 | 2 | $2 \times 2$ |
| 2D Quadratic Q8/Q9 | 2 | 4 | $3 \times 3$ |
| 4-node Tetrahedron | 1 | 2 | 1 (volume rule) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Reduced Integration (KTU Favorite Trick):** Using *fewer* Gauss points than the minimum required (e.g., $1 \times 1$ for Q4 instead of $2 \times 2$) is called *reduced integration*. It lowers computational cost and can suppress parasitic stiffness, but risks **hourglass / zero-energy modes** if overdone.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Print & Carry)

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Domain** |
|:---|:---|:---:|
| $\int_{a}^{b} f\,dx \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i\, f(x_i)$ | General quadrature form | Generic |
| $\dfrac{h}{2}[f_0 + f_1]$ | Trapezoidal rule | 1D |
| $\dfrac{h}{3}[f_0 + 4f_1 + f_2]$ | Simpson's 1/3 rule | 1D |
| $\dfrac{3h}{8}[f_0 + 3f_1 + 3f_2 + f_3]$ | Simpson's 3/8 rule | 1D |
| $\sum W_i W_j\, f(\xi_i, \eta_j)$ | 2D tensor product Gauss | 2D |
| $\text{Exact for polynomials of degree} \leq 2n-1$ | Gauss accuracy property | All |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: 2-Point Gauss–Legendre Rule from First Principles

We seek constants $\xi_1, \xi_2, W_1, W_2$ such that the rule

$$\int_{-1}^{+1} f(\xi)\, d\xi \;\approx\; W_1 f(\xi_1) + W_2 f(\xi_2)$$

integrates the four monomials $1, \xi, \xi^{2}, \xi^{3}$ **exactly**.

**Step 1 — Impose exactness for $f(\xi) = 1$:**

$$\int_{-1}^{+1} 1\, d\xi \;=\; 2 \;=\; W_1 + W_2$$

**Step 2 — Impose exactness for $f(\xi) = \xi$:**

$$\int_{-1}^{+1} \xi\, d\xi \;=\; 0 \;=\; W_1 \xi_1 + W_2 \xi_2$$

**Step 3 — Impose exactness for $f(\xi) = \xi^{2}$:**

$$\int_{-1}^{+1} \xi^{2}\, d\xi \;=\; \frac{2}{3} \;=\; W_1 \xi_1^{2} + W_2 \xi_2^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Impose exactness for $f(\xi) = \xi^{3}$:**

$$\int_{-1}^{+1} \xi^{3}\, d\xi \;=\; 0 \;=\; W_1 \xi_1^{3} + W_2 \xi_2^{3}$$

**Step 5 — Use symmetry to simplify.** Because $[-1,+1]$ is symmetric about 0, and the weights must be symmetric, we set:

$$\xi_2 = -\xi_1, \quad W_1 = W_2$$

**Step 6 — Apply to Step 1:**

$$2W_1 = 2 \;\Longrightarrow\; W_1 = W_2 = 1$$

**Step 7 — Apply to Step 3 (after substituting $W_1 = W_2 = 1$ and $\xi_2 = -\xi_1$):**

$$1 \cdot \xi_1^{2} + 1 \cdot (-\xi_1)^{2} \;=\; \frac{2}{3} \;\Longrightarrow\; 2\xi_1^{2} = \frac{2}{3} \;\Longrightarrow\; \xi_1^{2} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\xi_1 = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \quad \xi_2 = +\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \quad W_1 = W_2 = 1}$$

**Verification — Apply the rule to a degree-3 polynomial** $f(\xi) = 2\xi^{3} + \xi^{2} - 3\xi + 5$:

$$W_1 f(\xi_1) + W_2 f(\xi_2) = 1\cdot f(-1/\sqrt{3}) + 1\cdot f(1/\sqrt{3})$$

Because $f$ contains a degree-3 term, $f(-x) = -2x^{3} + x^{2} + 3x + 5$, so the cubic components cancel, leaving:

$$f(-1/\sqrt{3}) + f(1/\sqrt{3}) \;=\; 2\xi^{2} + 10 \;=\; 2 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} + 10 = \tfrac{32}{3}$$

The exact integral is:

$$\int_{-1}^{+1} (2\xi^{3} + \xi^{2} - 3\xi + 5)\, d\xi = 0 + \tfrac{2}{3} - 0 + 10 = \tfrac{32}{3} \;\checkmark$$

The rule is **exact for degree 3**, as predicted by the $2n-1$ formula.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Trapezoidal Rule (1D, 2 stations)

**Problem:** Evaluate $\int_{0}^{2} (x^{2} + 1)\, dx$ using the trapezoidal rule with 2 points.

**Step 1 — Discretize the domain:** $h = (2 - 0) = 2$.

**Step 2 — Identify stations:** $x_0 = 0, \; x_1 = 2$.

**Step 3 — Evaluate the function:**

$$f(x_0) = f(0) = 0^{2} + 1 = 1$$

$$f(x_1) = f(2) = 2^{2} + 1 = 5$$

**Step 4 — Apply the trapezoidal formula:**

$$I \;\approx\; \frac{h}{2}\bigl[\,f(x_0) + f(x_1)\,\bigr] \;=\; \frac{2}{2}(1 + 5) \;=\; 6$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the exact value:**

$$I_{\text{exact}} = \int_{0}^{2} (x^{2}+1)\, dx = \tfrac{x^{3}}{3} + x \;\Big|_{0}^{2} = \tfrac{8}{3} + 2 = \tfrac{14}{3} \approx 4.6667$$

**Step 6 — Compute the error:**

$$E = \vert\, I_{\text{approx}} - I_{\text{exact}} \,\vert = \vert\, 6 - 4.6667 \,\vert = 1.3333$$

This is consistent with the error formula $E = -\dfrac{(b-a)^{3}}{12} f''(\xi) = -\dfrac{8}{12}(2) = -\tfrac{4}{3}$.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Simpson's 1/3 Rule (1D, 3 stations)

**Problem:** Evaluate $\int_{0}^{4} e^{x}\, dx$ using Simpson's 1/3 rule with 3 points.

**Step 1 — Discretize the domain:** $h = \dfrac{4 - 0}{2} = 2$.

**Step 2 — Identify stations:** $x_0 = 0, \; x_1 = 2, \; x_2 = 4$.

**Step 3 — Evaluate the function:**

$$f(x_0) = e^{0} = 1.0000$$

$$f(x_1) = e^{2} = 7.3891$$

$$f(x_2) = e^{4} = 54.5982$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Simpson's 1/3 formula:**

$$I \;\approx\; \frac{h}{3}\bigl[\,f(x_0) + 4 f(x_1) + f(x_2)\,\bigr] \;=\; \frac{2}{3}\bigl[\,1 + 4(7.3891) + 54.5982\,\bigr]$$

$$= \frac{2}{3}\bigl[\,1 + 29.5564 + 54.5982\,\bigr] = \frac{2}{3}(85.1546) = 56.7697$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the exact value:**

$$I_{\text{exact}} = \int_{0}^{4} e^{x}\, dx = e^{4} - e^{0} = 54.5982 - 1 = 53.5982$$

**Step 6 — Compute the error:**

$$E = \vert\, 56.7697 - 53.5982 \,\vert = 3.1715$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Simpson's rule over-estimated because $e^{x}$ is convex upward (positive fourth derivative), making the error $E = -\dfrac{(b-a)^{5}}{180} f^{(4)}(\xi)$ positive in magnitude with the negative sign indicating over-estimation is *not* universal — the sign depends on the fourth derivative.

## 3.4 Worked Example — 2-Point Gauss Quadrature (Mapped Domain)

**Problem:** Evaluate $I = \int_{0}^{3} (x^{2} + 2x)\, dx$ using the 2-point Gauss rule.

**Step 1 — Map the domain $[0, 3]$ to the natural domain $[-1, +1]$ using:**

$$x = \frac{b-a}{2}\xi + \frac{b+a}{2} = \frac{3}{2}\xi + \frac{3}{2} = \frac{3}{2}(\xi + 1)$$

$$dx = \frac{3}{2}\, d\xi$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the integral:**

$$I = \int_{-1}^{+1} \left[\left(\tfrac{3}{2}(\xi+1)\right)^{2} + 2 \cdot \tfrac{3}{2}(\xi+1) \right] \cdot \tfrac{3}{2}\, d\xi$$

$$= \frac{3}{2}\int_{-1}^{+1} \left[\tfrac{9}{4}(\xi+1)^{2} + 3(\xi+1) \right] d\xi$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 2-point Gauss rule** with $\xi_1 = -1/\sqrt{3}, \; \xi_2 = +1/\sqrt{3}, \; W_1 = W_2 = 1$:

$$I \approx \frac{3}{2}\bigl[\,1 \cdot g(\xi_1) + 1 \cdot g(\xi_2)\,\bigr]$$

where $g(\xi) = \tfrac{9}{4}(\xi+1)^{2} + 3(\xi+1)$.

**Step 4 — Evaluate $g$ at the two stations:**

At $\xi_1 = -1/\sqrt{3} \approx -0.5774$:

$$g(\xi_1) = \tfrac{9}{4}(0.4226)^{2} + 3(0.4226) = 0.4018 + 1.2678 = 1.6696$$

At $\xi_2 = +1/\sqrt{3} \approx +0.5774$:

$$g(\xi_2) = \tfrac{9}{4}(1.5774)^{2} + 3(1.5774) = 5.5992 + 4.7322 = 10.3314$$

**Step 5 — Sum and multiply by the Jacobian factor:**

$$I \approx \frac{3}{2}\bigl[\,1.6696 + 10.3314\,\bigr] = \frac{3}{2}(12.0010) = 18.0015$$

**Step 6 — Compare with the exact value:**

$$I_{\text{exact}} = \int_{0}^{3} (x^{2} + 2x)\, dx = \tfrac{x^{3}}{3} + x^{2} \;\Big|_{0}^{3} = 9 + 9 = 18$$

The 2-point Gauss rule recovered the exact answer because the integrand is a polynomial of degree 2, well within the rule's exactness threshold of degree 3.

## 3.5 Worked Example — 2-Point Gauss in 2D (4-Stations)

**Problem:** Evaluate $I = \int_{0}^{1}\!\!\int_{0}^{1} (x + 2y)\, dx\, dy$ using a $2 \times 2$ Gauss product rule.

**Step 1 — Map $[0,1]\times[0,1]$ to $[-1,1]\times[-1,1]$:**

$$x = \tfrac{1}{2}(\xi+1), \quad y = \tfrac{1}{2}(\eta+1), \quad dx\,dy = \tfrac{1}{4}\,d\xi\,d\eta$$

**Step 2 — Set up the 4-station product rule:**

$$I \approx \tfrac{1}{4}\sum_{i=1}^{2}\sum_{j=1}^{2} W_i W_j \bigl[\,x(\xi_i) + 2 y(\eta_j)\,\bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Tabulate the four stations:**

| **Station** | $\xi_i$ | $\eta_j$ | $x$ | $y$ | $f = x + 2y$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| (1,1) | $-0.5774$ | $-0.5774$ | $0.2113$ | $0.2113$ | $0.6339$ |
| (1,2) | $-0.5774$ | $+0.5774$ | $0.2113$ | $0.7887$ | $1.7887$ |
| (2,1) | $+0.5774$ | $-0.5774$ | $0.7887$ | $0.2113$ | $1.2113$ |
| (2,2) | $+0.5774$ | $+0.5774$ | $0.7887$ | $0.7887$ | $2.3661$ |

**Step 4 — Apply the product rule (all weights = 1):**

$$I \approx \tfrac{1}{4}\bigl[\,0.6339 + 1.7887 + 1.2113 + 2.3661\,\bigr] = \tfrac{1}{4}(6.0000) = 1.5000$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the exact value:**

$$I_{\text{exact}} = \int_{0}^{1}\!\!\int_{0}^{1} (x + 2y)\, dx\, dy = \tfrac{1}{2} + 2 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} = 1.5 \;\checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the 2D rule gives the exact answer:** The integrand is a polynomial of total degree 1 in $(x, y)$, and the $2 \times 2$ rule is exact for polynomials up to total degree 3.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: Numerical Integration Pipeline in FEM

The diagram below traces the lifecycle of a typical element-stiffness integral as it passes through the numerical-integration engine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Element Stiffness Integral in Physical Coordinates: ∫[B]T[D][B] dV"] --> B["Apply Isoparametric Mapping: x = N x_node, y = N y_node"]
    B --> C["Obtain Jacobian Matrix: [J] = ∂(x,y) / ∂(ξ,η)"]
    C --> D["Transform Integral to Parent Domain: ∫[B]T[D][B] · det J dξ dη"]
    D --> E["Choose Gauss Order n: requires 2n - 1 ≥ integrand degree"]
    E --> F["Load Gauss Sampling Table: ξ_i, η_i, W_i, W_j"]
    F --> G["Loop over Gauss Stations i, j"]
    G --> H["Evaluate B Matrix at Station"]
    H --> I["Compute Stiffness Contribution: W_i W_j det J · BT D B"]
    I --> J["Assemble into Element Matrix K_e"]
    J --> K{"More Stations?"}
    K -- Yes --> G
    K -- No --> L["Final Element Stiffness Matrix [K]^e"]
    L --> M["Assemble into Global System [K]{U} = {F}"]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree: Choosing Gauss Order in FEM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Identify Element Shape Functions N_i"] --> B["Determine Polynomial Order p of N_i"]
    B --> C["Integrand Polynomial Degree = 2p"]
    C --> D{"Integrand is in 1D / 2D / 3D?"}
    D -- "1D" --> E["Choose Gauss order n ≥ p + 1"]
    D -- "2D" --> F["Use n × n product rule, n ≥ p + 1"]
    D -- "3D" --> G["Use n × n × n product rule, n ≥ p + 1"]
    E --> H["Verify: 2n - 1 ≥ 2p"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H -- "Satisfied" --> I["Select Station Coordinates & Weights"]
    H -- "Not Satisfied" --> J["Increase n and Recompute"]
    I --> K{"Reduced Integration Desired?"}
    K -- "Yes" --> L["Use n - 1 order; check for hourglass"]
    K -- "No" --> M["Use full integration; proceed"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Integration Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Layer"]
        I1["Integrand Function f(ξ,η)"]
        I2["Element Geometry: Nodes, Connectivity"]
        I3["Material Law: D Matrix"]
    end

    subgraph CORE["Integration Engine"]
        C1["Gauss Point Generator"]

    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Layer"]

    end

    I1 --> C2
    I2 --> C1
    I2 --> C2
    I2 --> C4
    I3 --> C3
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C2 --> C4
    C3 --> C5
    C4 --> C5
    C1 --> C5
    C5 --> O1
    C5 --> O2
    C5 --> O3
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Numerical Integration Methods"] --> B["Newton-Cotes Family"]
    A --> C["Gauss Family"]
```
